Scope: - In-app messaging between users and external contacts - Business messaging over carrier networks (SMS/MMS, A2P) including configuration and controls at the admin level - Messaging reliability and routing tightly coupled to core app behavior (e.g., message delivery expectations that end users perceive directly)

The Omnichannel team (Support) owns agent and supervisor workflows across digital channels. Connect focuses on the core messaging fabric and UX inside Dialpad and related admin controls.

docs.title: 2.4 Hardware
docs.text:
Hardware covers the endpoints that bring Dialpad and Dialpad Meetings into physical spaces (desk phones, room systems, and certified devices).

Scope: - Desk phones and meeting room hardware that integrate with Dialpad telephony and meetings - Provisioning flows, including zero-touch provisioning where supported - Bulk device management from admin surfaces (assignment, updates, deprovisioning, and monitoring)

Hardware depends heavily on Telephony & Carrier Platform for PSTN connectivity, reliability, and failover. Connect owns the device experience and management flows seen by admins and end users.

docs.title: 2.5 Admin (Core App & Admin)
docs.text:
Admin covers the administrative experience for configuring and operating the core communications stack: users, numbers, devices, policies, and core product settings across Connect surfaces.

Scope: - Core admin UX in the Dialpad app: navigation, information architecture, and workflows for managing offices, users, and features - RBAC and delegation for Connect-related resources (e.g., admin vs. office vs. department scopes in communications management) - Partner and support portals where they directly expose Connect capabilities (configuration of calling, meetings, messaging, devices) to external operators or partners

The broader identity, billing, analytics, and growth building blocks are provided by Shared Services and other core areas. Admin in Connect focuses on how these levers are surfaced and managed in the product UI.

docs.title: 3. Boundaries with Other Core Areas
docs.text:
Connect does not work in isolation. The table below clarifies where Connect ends and other areas begin.

vs. Connect owns Other area owns Support Core communications experience (calling, meetings, messaging, devices, admin) for knowledge workers Contact center capabilities (queues, agent/supervisor desktops, omnichannel routing, WFM, AI for service interactions) Sell Horizontal communications for the entire organization Sales engagement workflows (dialers, sales-specific AI assistance, performance management) built on top of the core communications fabric Ecosystem Core app behaviors and experiences for communications Integrations (CRM, vertical systems, developer platform) that Connect consumes to enrich the user experience Shared Services How billing, pricing, analytics, and self-service constructs show up in the communications UI Underlying billing, pricing, analytics platforms, and monetization infrastructure used by all core areas Agentic AI Platform Surfaces agentic behaviors (Daart) within calls, meetings, and messaging Autonomous agents, agent studio, orchestration, and optimization of agentic workloads across products

docs.title: 4. Experience & Quality Principles
docs.text: These principles apply consistently across Daart, Meetings, Messaging, Hardware, and Admin.

docs.title: Reliability by default
docs.text: Telephony, meetings, messaging, and devices should behave like a dependable utility — available, predictable, and observable. Quality work spans from core media paths and carrier routing to device behavior and admin-facing monitoring.

docs.title: Unified communications, single platform
docs.text: Users should experience one app that combines phones, messaging, meetings, and digital channels — not a collection of loosely coupled tools. Connect owns the cohesion of this experience within the core apps.

docs.title: AI-native but explainable surfaces
docs.text: Agentic and AI features (Daart, summaries, AI assistance) should feel embedded, useful, and trustworthy — not experimental add-ons. Admins must be able to understand and control how AI behaves in their environment (configuration, compliance toggles, visibility into what AI did).

docs.title: Admin-first operability
docs.text: Admin experiences should make it simple to deploy, change, and troubleshoot communications for an organization, including devices and policies. Connect's Admin and Hardware surfaces are key to reducing operational friction and strengthening overall platform quality.

docs.title: 5. What Connect Is Not
docs.text:
• Not a standalone contact center solution — Connect provides the underlying communications fabric that Support builds on for queues, agent desktops, and omnichannel orchestration.
  • Not a full sales engagement platform — Connect underpins Sell with calling, meetings, and messaging but does not own sales-specific workflows or performance analytics.
  • Not trying to mirror every feature of external telephony or meetings products — differentiation is through a unified, AI-native communications platform, not feature-for-feature mimicry.

docs.title: 6. Related Context Files
docs.text: File Scope DP-General-mission-and-strategy.md Company-level mission, all Core Areas, FY27 imperatives DP-General-competitive-positioning.md Full competitive landscape including UCaaS competitors Support-context.md Contact center context; clarifies Connect vs. Support boundaries

docs.title: 7. How to Use This File
docs.text:
• PRDs: Load this file when drafting any artifact related to Daart, Meetings, Messaging, Hardware, or Admin within the Connect Core Area.
  • Boundary clarification: Use Section 3 to resolve scope questions when a feature could belong to Connect, Support, Sell, or Ecosystem.
  • Quality & principles: Use Section 4 to validate that a proposed design or feature direction aligns with Connect's stated principles before writing requirements.
  • Refresh cadence: Update after each EPD QBR and after any significant boundary or ownership change between core areas.

docs.title: Ecosystem Core Area — Grounding Context
docs.text: Ecosystem is not a standalone Dialpad product — it is the connective tissue that links all Dialpad products into the rest of a customer's software environment. Its strategic purpose is to position Dialpad as a platform rather than a closed application, enabling customers, partners, and developers to extend and adapt Dialpad through integrations, APIs, and AI-connected tooling.

docs.title: 1. What the Ecosystem Core Area Is
docs.text:
The Ecosystem core area enables customers to extend and adapt Dialpad through external APIs, integrations, and related tools — so that communications and AI capabilities can plug into the broader software stack they already use.

Ecosystem focuses on two primary dimensions:

  • Integrations: Opinionated, productized connections between Dialpad and key third-party systems across CRM, helpdesk, productivity, identity, and data platforms.
  • Developer platform: The APIs, events, webhooks, authentication model, and tooling that allow engineering teams and partners to build custom solutions on top of Dialpad.

Strategic outcome: Position Dialpad as a Platform — not a feature on someone else's platform.

docs.title: 2. Strategic Role in the Product Suite
docs.text:
Ecosystem is a cross-cutting enabler: it does not own a standalone application, but rather the connective tissue that links Dialpad into the rest of a customer's environment. Its role is to:

  • Make Dialpad interoperable with the systems customers already rely on (CRM, ticketing, collaboration, identity, analytics)
  • Provide a coherent, extensible surface for AI and communications capabilities to be embedded into external tools and workflows
  • Enable partners and customers to tailor Dialpad to specific industries and use cases through vertical integrations and custom-built solutions

The general rule: when a feature involves external systems, partner tooling, or programmability, Ecosystem should own or co-own the underlying platform decisions.

docs.title: 3.1 Core Integrations Platform
docs.text: Shared infrastructure for configuring, authenticating, and managing integrations: - OAuth flows, token storage, connection health, error handling, rate limiting - Common patterns for mapping external entities (accounts, contacts, cases, opportunities, tickets, conversations) to Dialpad constructs (users, offices, calls, messages, agents, transcripts)

docs.title: 3.2 Productized Integrations
docs.text: First-party integrations with major platforms: - CRM & sales systems (SFDC, HubSpot): Surface Dialpad activity (calls, meetings, messages, summaries) inside the system of record; keep customer context synchronized both ways - Support & ticketing: Log interactions, link conversations to tickets or cases, and enable agents to see relevant context from within Dialpad's agent desktop - Productivity & collaboration suites: Calendar, email, file, and identity integrations that align meetings, contact data, and authentication with the broader productivity stack - No-code automation: Triggers based on Dialpad events + actions that push/pull data to/from external systems without writing code

docs.title: 3.3 Developer Platform
docs.text:
For engineering teams and partners:

Surface Description APIs Telephony, messaging, users & offices, contact center config, transcripts, analytics, AI/agent actions Webhooks & events Subscription-based model for calls, meetings, messages, AI outputs, and lifecycle changes in near-real time Patterns & docs Blueprints for embedded CTI, provisioning automation, custom BI/analytics, and agent-assist integrations

The intent: make Dialpad programmable — any entity or action that matters to customers should be accessible through stable, well-documented interfaces.

docs.title: 3.4 Agentic AI Ecosystem
docs.text:
As Agentic AI matures, Ecosystem increasingly owns the surface where AI connects to external tools:

Component Description Tool & connector registry Configuration for which external systems are available to agents, and under which permissions and scopes Connection management Authentication, secrets, rate limiting, and error recovery for tools invoked by AI agents Execution & observability Standard patterns for logging, auditing, and observing tool calls so AI-driven actions remain understandable and governable

docs.title: 4. Design & Operating Principles
docs.text: Principle What It Means Platform-first, application-neutral Design capabilities to serve multiple Dialpad products (Connect, Support, Sell, Meetings, Agentic AI), not a single app Security, privacy, and compliance by default Align with company-wide auth, authorization, data residency, and retention standards — integrations must not weaken overall posture Customer choice and interoperability Integrate with the tools customers already use; don't force a single all-in stack Configurability without fragmentation Flexible admin configuration, but consistent underlying data models — no one-off integrations that are hard to support AI-aware by design New integrations, APIs, and events must expose the right structure for AI use cases: high-quality transcripts, conversation metadata, outcomes, and contextual fields agents can safely consume

docs.title: 5. Boundaries with Other Core Areas
docs.text:
Ecosystem works horizontally across the other Core Areas:

Core Area How Ecosystem Relates Support & Sell Deep CRM, ticketing, and analytics integrations so customer-facing teams see full conversation context inside their tools of record; contact center and sales data flows into external reporting systems Connect & Meetings Calling, messaging, and meetings are made aware of calendar, email, identity, and collaboration context; call/meeting data can be embedded into external applications Shared Services Work together to define uniform cross-product data models and interfaces exposed to third parties — avoiding divergent schema per app Agentic AI Platform Ecosystem owns the external tool surface (tool registry, connection management, observability); Agentic AI Platform owns the orchestration and execution logic

docs.title: 6. Out of Scope
docs.text: Ecosystem does not own: - Core telephony, agent desktops, or end-user application UX (owned by respective product Core Areas) - Internal-only tooling or one-off integrations not intended for long-term support or GA (unless productized into the shared platform) - Company-level positioning, pricing, or commercial packaging decisions

docs.title: 7. Related Context Files
docs.text: File Scope DP-General-mission-and-strategy.md Company-level mission, all Core Areas, FY27 imperatives DP-General-competitive-positioning.md Integration competitive landscape (Genesys, Amazon Connect, Five9, Aircall) Connect-context.md Core app context; Ecosystem provides the external connectivity Connect consumes Support-context.md CCaaS context; Ecosystem owns the CRM/ticketing integrations used in agent workflows Sell-context.md Sales engagement context; Ecosystem owns the CRM integrations Sell depends on

docs.title: 8. How to Use This File
docs.text:
• PRDs: Load this file when drafting any artifact that touches integrations, external APIs, developer surfaces, webhooks, or AI tool ecosystems.
  • Boundary routing: Use Section 5 to determine whether a problem or proposal belongs in Ecosystem vs. a product Core Area.
  • Agentic AI work: Reference Section 3.4 when designing or reviewing features where AI agents interact with external systems.
  • Refresh cadence: Update after each EPD QBR and after any significant change to the integration platform, developer platform scope, or agentic tool ecosystem.

docs.title: Sell Core Area — Grounding Context
docs.text: Dialpad Sell is a core Dialpad product. It is one of three primary commercial products in the Dialpad suite — alongside Dialpad Connect (UCaaS) and Dialpad Support (CCaaS) — and is sold, marketed, and developed as a first-class product line under the Dialpad brand.

docs.title: 1. What Sell Is
docs.text:
Dialpad Sell is Dialpad's AI-powered sales engagement product, focused on helping revenue teams run high-volume, high-quality, communication-led workflows across outbound calling, live coaching, and team performance.

At its core, Sell owns the communication-centric parts of the sales workflow — not the full CRM, contact database, or end-to-end marketing automation stack.

Sell is built on the broader Dialpad platform (UCaaS + CCaaS, shared AI and data layer), so its value comes not just from point features but from how calling, conversation intelligence, coaching, and analytics work together.

Strategic outcome: Credible alternative to Gong.

docs.title: 2. Core Scope
docs.text:
Sell is centered on four connected product surfaces:

Surface Role Power Dialer Outbound dialing and campaign-based engagement at scale Playbooks Structured guidance and adherence tracking during conversations RTA (Real-Time Assist) In-call prompts and live assistance while the conversation is happening Performance Management Analytics and coaching views that help managers understand rep effectiveness and guide improvement

These areas are interdependent: Power Dialer increases outbound activity, Playbooks and RTA improve call execution in the moment, and Performance Management turns conversation data into coaching and operational insight.

docs.title: 3. Product Philosophy
docs.text:
Sell is a communication-led sales engagement layer. The product is strongest when it helps teams place more calls, have better conversations, and turn live interactions into actionable insight for reps and managers.

Intentionally in scope: - Scaling outbound engagement - Improving conversation quality in real time - Making account and team context more actionable - Exposing analytics that help teams understand what is working and what needs coaching

Intentionally out of scope: - Replacing systems of record (CRM, contact databases) - Building broad workflow ownership across the entire sales stack - Feature-for-feature competition with Gong, ZoomInfo, or Outreach on contact management or cadence automation

docs.title: 4. Power Dialer
docs.text:
Power Dialer is the outbound execution engine inside Sell — connecting live calls to agents at scale from large pools of dialable contacts and campaign workflows.

Core responsibilities: - Campaign creation and management - List and contact handling - Scaling outbound voice engagement - Expanding toward multi-touch and multi-channel workflows - Creating visibility into campaign outcomes inside analytics

Power Dialer also acts as a bridge between classic outbound workflows and newer automation patterns, including agentic outbound use cases and tighter campaign orchestration across channels.

FY27 priority: Power Dialer GA is a Q1 non-negotiable. This is the most critical near-term Sell deliverable.

docs.title: 5. Playbooks
docs.text:
Playbooks map desired talk tracks, qualification frameworks, or process steps into the live conversation experience — providing structured call guidance and making adherence visible during and after calls.

Their job is not just to display guidance, but to make adherence measurable so reps, managers, and analytics surfaces can understand how well conversations followed the intended approach.

Playbooks become most valuable when connected to RTA, summaries, analytics, and team coaching workflows — not when operating as a standalone checklist.

docs.title: 6. RTA (Real-Time Assist)
docs.text:
RTA is the in-call assist layer that surfaces guidance during live interactions. Its role is to reduce the gap between what a rep should do and what they are actually doing in the moment — through prompts, suggestions, and context-aware assistance while the conversation is still happening.

RTA sits alongside Playbooks as part of the real-time coaching experience. Current direction points toward a simpler, more unified assistant-style experience rather than many fragmented coaching surfaces.

docs.title: 7. Performance Management
docs.text:
Performance Management helps managers move from individual calls to repeatable coaching and team-level improvement. It depends on conversation data, live coaching signals, and analytics to answer:

  • How are reps performing?
  • Where is coaching most needed?
  • Which behaviors correlate with stronger outcomes?
  • How does team effectiveness change over time?

This area is not only about reporting — it is about turning raw conversation intelligence into manager workflows: review, compare, coach, and improve.

docs.title: 8. How the Four Areas Work Together
docs.text:
These four surfaces should be read as one connected system:

Power Dialer  →  creates more sales conversations
Playbooks     →  defines what good execution looks like in those conversations
RTA           →  helps reps execute better while the call is happening
Perf. Mgmt    →  helps managers review patterns across calls and coach the team

Sell is not a dialer plus AI add-ons. It is an outbound and coaching system built around live conversations, guided execution, and manager visibility.

docs.title: 9. Boundaries with Adjacent Areas
docs.text:
Adjacent area How Sell relates What Sell does not own Dialpad Platform (Connect) Relies on shared calling, transcription, and conversation data Core telephony infrastructure, messaging fabric, admin UX Shared Services (Analytics) Benefits from shared analytics platform for reporting consistency Analytics platform infrastructure, cross-product data layer Ecosystem (CRM) Connects to CRM context (SFDC, HubSpot) for account enrichment CRM system of record, contact database, sequencing automation Agentic AI Platform Intersects on outbound automation and proactive assistance Autonomous agent infrastructure, agent studio, orchestration

This boundary is important because it keeps Sell focused on the part of the workflow where Dialpad is most differentiated: the live communication layer and the intelligence generated from it.

docs.title: 10. Related Context Files
docs.text: File Scope DP-General-mission-and-strategy.md Company-level mission, all Core Areas, FY27 imperatives DP-General-competitive-positioning.md Sales engagement competitive landscape (Gong, Outreach, ZoomInfo) Connect-context.md Core communications platform that Sell is built on top of

docs.title: 11. How to Use This File
docs.text:
Load this file when working on artifacts related to: - Outbound sales engagement - Real-time sales coaching - Call guidance and adherence - Rep and manager performance workflows - Sell-specific analytics or positioning

Applicable artifact types: PRDs, strategy docs, roadmap discussions, task breakdowns, GTM artifacts — especially when the work involves Power Dialer, Playbooks, RTA, or Performance Management.

Refresh cadence: Update after each EPD QBR and after any significant scope or ownership change affecting the Sell core area.

docs.title: Shared Services Core Area — Grounding Context
docs.text: Shared Services is not a standalone Dialpad product — it is the portfolio's common systems layer for commercial logic, activation flows, and measurement. It does not own an end-user application surface; instead it provides the foundational capabilities that make all other Dialpad products easier to buy, easier to activate, easier to measure, and easier to operate consistently.

docs.title: 1. What Shared Services Is
docs.text:
Shared Services centralizes foundational capabilities that would otherwise be rebuilt independently inside multiple product areas. These capabilities are exposed to other teams through shared services and APIs so feature teams can focus on product-specific workflows while relying on common platform primitives.

This file covers the four Shared Services domains: Billing, Growth, Pricing, and Analytics.

Strategic outcome: Provide industry-leading supporting services to all other Core Areas.

docs.title: 2. Why This Area Exists
docs.text:
As Dialpad expanded across multiple products, the same underlying needs kept appearing in different places — especially around purchasing, onboarding, reporting, and shared operational logic. Shared Services exists to:

  • Reduce duplicate work across Core Areas
  • Improve consistency across products
  • Create deep expertise in inherently complex domains (billing logic, experimentation frameworks, data pipelines)

The result is a more scalable product and engineering model where common flows become more reliable, more consistent, and easier for other teams to adopt.

docs.title: 3. Core Philosophy
docs.text:
"The core philosophy of Shared Services is to expose services that unblock other teams rather than becoming a gatekeeper for every implementation detail."

In practice: Shared Services provides reusable levers, configuration, and infrastructure so product teams can move with minimal direct involvement from the shared team after the primitives are in place. This model favors standardization where it improves consistency, while preserving enough flexibility for product teams to build differentiated customer experiences on top.

docs.title: 4.1 Billing
docs.text:
Billing is the shared system layer supporting subscriptions, usage-based services, credits, invoicing, purchase changes, renewals, and contract-linked lifecycle mechanics across Dialpad products.

Key structural concepts:

Concept Description SKU vs. Pricing SKUs define what the customer is buying; Pricing defines how much they pay based on currency, billing cycle, office context, payment method, and negotiated terms The office as billing unit Each office has exactly one plan; billing happens at the office level even when multiple offices belong to the same company Independent vs. unified billing Offices can bill independently (own cycles, currencies, terms) or be unified under the main office (all follow main office's billing rules) Service models Pay-as-you-go (no reset), monthly pooled entitlements/fair-use bundles, pre-purchased commitments renewing monthly or per billing cycle Metering Billing interprets metering units (minutes, pages, messages, sessions, conversations), applies reset logic, and determines how overages or downstream consumption are handled

Credits: - Usage-based charges deduct from a customer credit balance - Recurring credit packages function like commitments that renew with the billing cycle and provide discounted usage across eligible services - Current direction: moving from office-isolated credit behavior toward company-level credit consumption, where entitlements remain office-specific but credits are managed centrally at the company/primary-office level

Contract billing: - Contracted customers are often managed in NetSuite with offices grouped for billing purposes — internal groupings not always visible in the product admin experience

The billing situation is a coordination problem across catalog structure, pricing rules, office hierarchy, credit logic, metering, invoicing, and contract systems. The area stays tightly coupled to quote-to-bill consistency, invoice visibility, usage transparency, and reducing manual intervention caused by fragmented configuration or mismatched system behavior.

docs.title: 4.2 Growth
docs.text:
Growth is the reusable product layer for onboarding, discovery, signup, experimentation, upsell, cross-sell, fraud controls, and buying flow infrastructure.

Its job: give product teams common frameworks for user activation and in-product commercial moments without requiring each team to rebuild those systems from scratch.

Operating direction: Shift from one-off feature ownership toward reusable platform services — onboarding frameworks, sign-up flows, experimentation infrastructure, and feature flagging support.

docs.title: 4.3 Pricing
docs.text:
Pricing, in the Shared Services context, is the architecture that connects packaging, value metrics, quoting logic, usage rules, and invoice outcomes into a coherent model. It sits close to Billing because the platform must support different pricing models, tiers, credits, proration rules, and usage semantics without forcing every product launch to reinvent the commercial logic.

Recurring theme: Pricing simplification — fewer mismatches between quoting, provisioning, billing, and invoicing, with a stronger connection between packaging decisions and the underlying platform rules.

docs.title: 4.4 Analytics
docs.text:
Analytics is the shared reporting and insight foundation for the product portfolio. Its role:

  • Provide standardized data models and trusted reporting foundations
  • Maintain a consistent visual language and common platform for customer-facing analytics across products
  • Make analytics easier for feature teams to adopt while keeping definitions, APIs, and reporting experiences consistent across surfaces

docs.title: 5. How the Four Domains Fit Together
docs.text:
Billing, Pricing, Growth, and Analytics are tightly connected because they shape the full customer arc — from discovery to purchase to activation to usage to understanding outcomes.

Growth      →  creates reusable entry and activation flows
Pricing     →  defines the commercial structure
Billing     →  executes entitlement and charge mechanics
Analytics   →  explains what happened through trustworthy reporting

This is especially important for products with usage-based behavior, self-service flows, credits, or cross-product upgrades — where customer understanding depends on all four layers behaving consistently.

docs.title: 6. Design Principles
docs.text: Principle What It Means Consistency over local reinvention Core flows (purchasing, onboarding, reporting, discovery) should feel consistent across products, not rebuilt differently in each area Platform first, not project first Build reusable services and frameworks many teams can adopt, rather than solving the same problem repeatedly as a one-off Domain logic belongs close to the platform Complex logic (billing rules, experimentation systems, analytics data models) benefits from centralized ownership — correctness and consistency matter as much as feature speed Trust matters Shared Services is a trust layer: customers and internal teams depend on it for accurate billing, understandable pricing, reliable analytics, and predictable purchase flows

docs.title: 7. Common Tensions
docs.text:
The central tension in Shared Services is standardization vs. team autonomy:

  • Too centralized → product teams get blocked on dependencies and coordination overhead
  • Too loosely governed → teams recreate their own billing logic, onboarding patterns, pricing rules, or reporting definitions → inconsistency and duplication

The intended balance: shared infrastructure with enough flexibility for product teams to configure and move independently.

docs.title: 8. Boundaries with Other Core Areas
docs.text: Core Area How Shared Services Relates Support Billing supports CCaaS tiers and add-ons; Analytics provides unified reporting across voice, digital, and AI Connect Growth supports UCaaS onboarding and activation flows; Billing handles Connect plan and add-on mechanics Sell Billing handles Power Dialer and sales engagement packaging; Analytics surfaces Sell performance data Ecosystem Pricing and Billing define the commercial constructs Ecosystem exposes through APIs and partner portals Agentic AI Billing enables usage-based billing for Agentic sessions; Growth supports self-service Agentic activation flows

docs.title: 9. Related Context Files
docs.text: File Scope DP-General-mission-and-strategy.md Company-level mission, all Core Areas, FY27 imperatives including Shared Services philosophy Support-context.md CCaaS packaging tiers and add-ons that Billing must support

docs.title: 10. How to Use This File
docs.text:
• PRDs: Load this file when drafting any artifact that touches billing, pricing, onboarding, self-service flows, experimentation, or analytics infrastructure.
  • Billing design: Reference Section 4.1 for the full mental model of office hierarchy, credit mechanics, and service model types before writing billing-related requirements.
  • Boundary routing: Use Section 8 to determine whether a problem belongs in Shared Services vs. a product Core Area.
  • Refresh cadence: Update after each EPD QBR and after any significant change to billing architecture, pricing model, or analytics platform scope.

docs.title: Support Core Area — Grounding Context
docs.text: Dialpad Support is a core Dialpad product. It is one of three primary commercial products in the Dialpad suite — alongside Dialpad Connect (UCaaS) and Dialpad Sell (sales engagement) — and is sold, marketed, and developed as a first-class product line under the Dialpad brand.

docs.title: 1. What Dialpad Support Is
docs.text:
Dialpad Support is Dialpad's AI-powered CCaaS (Contact Center as a Service) product. It provides omnichannel customer support with voice, digital engagement (chat, SMS, email, social), AI-driven coaching, automated quality management, and deep analytics — all unified with Dialpad Connect (UCaaS) in a single TrueCaaS platform.

One-liner: AI-powered contact center software with live AI coaching, real-time sentiment tracking, omnichannel engagement, and integrated analytics for customer support teams.

Strategic outcome: Strong alternative to Nice, Genesys, and Five9.

docs.title: Dialpad Support Bundles
docs.text: Tier CSR ID Description Key Included Features Essentials dpcc_bundle_essentials AI-powered CC for SMB support teams Digital Engagement (1 channel), basic AI routing, real-time transcription, summaries Advanced dpcc_bundle_advanced Enterprise CC with advanced AI + Live Coach, Quality Management (AI CSAT, AI Scorecards), Digital Engagement (all channels) Premium dpcc_bundle_premium Top-tier enterprise CC + WFM, full QM suite, Business Intelligence, all engagement channels

docs.title: FY27 Packaging Refresh (In Progress)
docs.text:
Moving from feature-by-feature add-ons to value-based "containers":

  • Quality Management (QM): AI CSAT + AI Scorecards
  • Live Coach: AI Custom Moments, RTA, real-time suggestions
  • WFM: Forecasting, scheduling, adherence

Essentials remains limited; Advanced gets QM + Live Coach included; Premium gets everything including WFM.

docs.title: Add-Ons (Support-Specific)
docs.text: Add-On Essentials Advanced Premium Live Coach (AI Custom Moments, RTA) Paid Included Included Quality Management (AI CSAT, Scorecards) Paid Included Included WFM Paid Paid Included Digital Engagement (1 Channel) Included Included Included Digital Engagement (All Channels) Paid Included Included Unlimited Domestic Minutes Paid Paid Paid Voicemail Drop CC Paid Paid Paid CC Local Dialing Paid Paid Paid BI Add-On Paid Paid Paid Recordings & Transcripts (1yr) Paid Paid Included

docs.title: Contact Center Core
docs.text:
• Routing: Longest idle, round robin, fixed order, random order, skills-based routing, queue priority
  • IVR: Advanced multi-level auto attendant with customizable open/closed/holiday hours routing
  • Queues: Hold queues with delay announcements, caller position, exit-to-voicemail, callback requests
  • Agent Management: On-duty/off-duty status, wrap-up time, dispositions, call waiting, agent screen capture
  • Supervisory Tools: Real-time monitoring (listen, barge, takeover), live transcriptions for supervisors, real-time dashboards, status alerts
  • Call Features: Call transfer, call parking (personal lines), manual and automatic recording
  • Capacity: Up to 500 contact centers per account; up to 25 simultaneous ring (expanding to 50+ for enterprise)

docs.title: Departments vs. Contact Centers
docs.text: Departments offer basic routing (longest idle, round robin, fixed order, simultaneous). Contact Centers add: skills-based routing, queue priority, advanced hold queue, real-time dashboards, alerts, CSAT, dispositions, wrap-up time, agent screen capture, and all AI features (RTA, Playbooks, AI CSAT, Scorecards, sentiment, custom moments).

docs.title: AI Features (Contact Center Only)
docs.text: Feature Description Real-Time Transcription Live transcription of calls with speaker diarization AI Recaps Post-call summaries with action items AI CSAT Survey-free inferred customer satisfaction scoring AI Scorecards Automated/accelerated QA reviews AI Playbooks Real-time adherence tracking for defined call flows Real-Time Assist (RTA) Triggered guidance cards during live interactions AI Live Coach Real-time coaching overlay with custom moments and suggestions Custom Moments Keyword/phrase tracking for compliance and coaching Live Call Sentiment Real-time sentiment analysis during calls Speech Coaching Talk-to-listen ratio, filler words, talk speed AI Voice Isolation Background noise suppression

docs.title: Omnichannel / Digital Engagement
docs.text:
• Channels: WebChat, SMS/MMS, Email, Facebook, Instagram, X/Twitter, WhatsApp
  • Agent Desktop: Unified workspace for voice + digital with shared customer history
  • Agentic AI: GA autonomous voice + chat agents with human handoff
  • Digital Engagement Licensing: One-channel or all-channels add-on

docs.title: Analytics
docs.text:
• Canned Reports: Call history, queue health, callbacks, Power Dialer, agent productivity, AI agent analytics
  • Real-Time Dashboard: Live wallboard with queue stats, agent status, SLA metrics
  • Scheduled Reporting: Automated report delivery
  • Playlists: Curated call recording collections for coaching
  • Key Metrics: Wait time, service level, abandon threshold, disposition usage, CSAT results, agent status
  • AI Analytics: Agentic AI performance (deflection rate, success rate, AHT comparison, ROI calculator)

docs.title: Workforce Management (WFM)
docs.text:
• Forecasting: Call volume prediction
  • Scheduling: Automated agent schedule generation
  • Adherence Tracking: Real-time adherence monitoring (market-leading capability)
  • Shrinkage Management: Account for breaks, training, admin time
  • FY27 Pivot: Moving from planning-oriented features to real-time workforce optimization — intraday reforecasting, agent redeployment, dynamic response to actual conditions
  • Agentic Integration: WFM calculates how many human agents are needed based on actual AI agent performance
  • Target: $7.6M ARR

docs.title: Overarching Vision
docs.text: Treat Support + Analytics + AI as a single, cohesive platform investment — not a collection of disconnected features. Customers evaluate Support as an operational platform, not individual features.

docs.title: Pillar 1: Scalable Contact Center Foundations
docs.text:
• Advanced multi-skill routing model (replace simplistic skills-based routing that creates CC explosion)
  • Expand phone number limit per CC
  • Skill-based routing improvements
  • More simultaneous ring capacity
  • Merged identity: single customer profile across voice, SMS, email, chat channels
  • Scheduled callbacks improvements

docs.title: Pillar 2: Unified, Actionable Analytics
docs.text:
• Unified analytics with consistent definitions across voice and digital
  • Design analytics for personas (agent, supervisor, executive), not as raw data dumps
  • Agentic AI observability: closed feedback loop where insights drive improvement
  • Cross-channel AI insights

docs.title: Pillar 3: Trustworthy, Global AI
docs.text:
• PII redaction GA across transcripts and audio with full admin controls
  • Multilingual AI parity: 10+ languages with automatic language detection
  • AI CSAT expansion to outbound, direct numbers, and digital interactions
  • AI insight that surfaces conversation trends and root causes

docs.title: Pillar 4: Innovation with AI
docs.text:
• LLM-powered scorecards
  • Prescriptive recommended actions from AI insights
  • Agentic AI analytics (deflection rate, success rate, AHT benchmarks, ROI)

docs.title: 5. Key Metrics & Targets
docs.text: Metric Overall Support ARR Enterprise Support ARR Feature adoption (CSAT, Scorecards) Feature-related escalations Enterprise churn Omnichannel customers Digital sessions Digital Agents ARR Billable Sessions ARR WFM ARR

docs.title: Q4 FY26 Win/Loss (Contact Center)
docs.text:
• Average lost deal is 2–3x the won deal — enterprise gap is real
  • Product-controllable loss themes: concurrent licensing, predictive dialer depth, MS Teams integration, BPO multi-tenancy, omnichannel email queueing

Thesis: "We win and retain on value, not feature parity; customers who see better quality and efficiency outcomes are stickier and expand more."

docs.title: 6. Known Gaps & Limitations
docs.text: Gap Impact Status Concurrent licensing Enterprise deals lost Under evaluation Predictive/power dialer depth Lost to Five9, Genesys Power Dialer GA in Q1 FY27 MS Teams integration depth Lost to competitors Roadmap item BPO multi-tenancy Blocks BPO market segment Under evaluation Omnichannel email queueing Lost to Zoom, Five9 FY27 priority Skills-based routing simplicity CC explosion problem at scale Pillar 1 priority Phone number limit per CC (30) Blocks enterprise marketing use cases Expanding to ~200 Identity fragmentation Same customer appears as multiple contacts across channels Merged identity in FY27 AI language coverage Limited non-English support 10+ languages target POC instability 90% of wins require live POC; instability is top deal-killer Reliability investments

docs.title: 7. Competitive Context (Support-Specific)
docs.text: Competitor Threat Support-Specific Notes Genesys 🔴 High Enterprise depth, WEM suite, integration strength (Salesforce+ServiceNow), token-based AI pricing NICE CXone 🔴 High Breadth (WFM, QM, recording), Enlighten AI suite, Cognigy acquisition for agentic Five9 🟠 Medium Strong outbound/dialer, analyst recognition, lacks simultaneous ring Talkdesk 🟠 Medium Digital engagement, industry packages Zoom 🟠 Medium Price competitive, grabbing CCaaS churners Nextiva 🟠 Medium Enterprise push, winning some table-stakes RFPs

docs.title: Where Support Wins
docs.text:
• AI native and included in base (not token-based or add-on)
  • Unified UCaaS+CCaaS (TrueCaaS)
  • Real-time coaching during live calls
  • Faster deployment (2 months vs. 12 months with Genesys)
  • AI CSAT (survey-free) — unique differentiator

docs.title: 8. Agentic AI in Support Context
docs.text:
Agentic AI is both a standalone product and a capability within Support:

  • AI Agents handle the majority of support requests; humans focus on edge cases
  • Human transfer is positioned as a trust-building feature, not a failure
  • Agentic AI Agents escalate to human agents on the same platform seamlessly
  • WFM calculates human agent needs based on actual AI agent performance
  • Analytics track AI vs. human performance side-by-side (deflection, FCR, AHT, CSAT)

docs.title: Key Agentic Metrics
docs.text: Metric Description Deflection rate Contained vs. escalated interactions Success rate Deflection × CSAT AI AHT vs. Human AHT Average handle time comparison Skills usage Skill gap identification ROI calculator Agent hours saved, cost per interaction

docs.title: 9. Related Context Files
docs.text: File Scope DP-General-mission-and-strategy.md Company-level mission, all Core Areas, FY27 imperatives DP-General-competitive-positioning.md Full competitive landscape, win/loss patterns, AI feature matrix

docs.title: 10. How to Use This File
docs.text:
• PRDs: Load this file when drafting any Support, CC, Omnichannel, WFM, or AI+Support PRD to ground requirements in actual capabilities, packaging, and strategic direction.
  • Competitive analysis: For Support-specific competitive context; pair with DP-General-competitive-positioning.md for the full landscape.
  • Packaging/pricing work: For current tier definitions, add-on eligibility, and FY27 repack direction.

docs.title: Cross-functional Templates
docs.text:
Standardized output shapes for JIRA epics and stories — used by PM, Design, and Engineering.

  • JIRA Epic Template

    Standard Dialpad JIRA epic format — objective, scope, acceptance criteria, and linked stories.

    View template

  • JIRA Story Template

    Standard Dialpad JIRA story format — user story, acceptance criteria, technical notes, and definition of done.

    View template

Using templates with Claude

Use the Generate JIRA Epic skill or Epic from PRD prompt to populate these templates automatically.

docs.title: JIRA Epic Template
docs.text: Instructions for AI agents: Use this template when generating JIRA epics from a PRD. Load 01_context/product-areas/[CoreArea]-context.md and the PRD before generating. Each epic should represent a shippable unit of work — typically one feature or feature phase. Do NOT generate more than one epic per AI session.

docs.title: Summary (JIRA Title)
docs.text:
[CoreArea] — [Feature Name]: [One-line outcome description]

Example: Connect — Intelligent Admin: Agentic AI-powered universal settings search

docs.title: What this epic delivers
docs.text: 2–3 sentences. What will users be able to do when this epic is complete? Frame as an outcome, not a task list.

docs.title: Why we're building it
docs.text: 1–2 sentences. Business driver or strategic priority. Reference FY27 priority if applicable.

docs.title: Who it affects
docs.text: Persona Impact [Persona 1] [Persona 2]

docs.title: Acceptance Criteria (Epic Level)
docs.text:
High-level criteria that define "done" for the entire epic. Detailed story-level criteria go in each story.

# Criterion E-AC-1 All stories in this epic are closed and QA-verified E-AC-2 Feature is behind feature flag and flag is confirmed working E-AC-3 [Core user flow] works end-to-end for [persona] in staging E-AC-4 No critical or high-severity bugs open at Gate 2 E-AC-5 Help Center article drafted and reviewed before GA

docs.title: Stories (Subtask Breakdown)
docs.text:
Each story must be independently executable by one engineer in ≤1 sprint. Do not include stories that cannot start until another is complete — sequence them correctly.

# Story Title Persona Priority Dependencies 1 [Story: specific unit of work] Must / Should / Nice None 2 Story 1 3 4 [Story: error states and edge cases] Must Story 1 5 [Story: tests and coverage] Must Stories 1–4

See 02_templates/cross-functional/DP-General-jira-story-template.md for individual story format.

docs.title: Technical Notes
docs.text: Field Value Feature flag name Provided by Engineering — do not guess API changes Yes / No — [describe if Yes] Data migration required Yes / No Breaking changes Yes / No Performance considerations

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text: Dependency Team Required by Status Confirmed / Pending

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text: Milestone Target Date Engineering kickoff Stories scoped and pointed Development complete QA sign-off Gate 2 (EAP) GA / Gate 3

docs.title: Links
docs.text: Type Link PRD Design Studio Gate 1 deck Related epics

docs.title: Labels
docs.text:
Use standard JIRA labels for the Core Area and quarter, e.g.: FY27Q1-[core-area], design-required

Agent validation checklist: - [ ] Epic outcome is stated in terms of user capability, not engineering task - [ ] All stories are independently scoped (no story blocks another without explicit sequencing) - [ ] Feature flag field is not guessed — left for Engineering to fill - [ ] Acceptance criteria are binary pass/fail - [ ] Dependencies confirmed against 01_context/product-areas/ — no invented teams or systems

docs.title: JIRA Story Template
docs.text: Instructions for AI agents: Use this template when breaking an epic into stories. Each story must be completable in a single engineering session (≤1 sprint, typically ≤3 days of work). If a story requires more, split it. Every acceptance criterion must be self-validatable by an agent or QA engineer — no ambiguity.

docs.title: Summary (JIRA Title)
docs.text:
[Verb]: [specific, scoped outcome]

Examples: - Implement: Agent on-duty toggle with RNA state tracking - Build: Credit balance API endpoint with isolated wallet support - Fix: Monitor All view not reflecting agent RNA state in real time

docs.title: User Story
docs.text: As a [persona], I want to [specific action or capability], so that [outcome / value].

docs.title: Context
docs.text: 1–2 sentences. Why this story exists. Which epic does it belong to? What comes before or after it?

docs.title: Scope
docs.text:
In scope: - Specific behavior 1 - Specific behavior 2

Out of scope (explicitly excluded): - Item 1 — deferred to [story #] or [later phase]

docs.title: Entry Criteria
docs.text:
What must be true BEFORE this story can start. All must be met — no exceptions.

  • [ ] [Prerequisite story] is merged and deployed to staging
  • [ ] Feature flag [flag_name] exists and is confirmed in CSR
  • [ ] Design Studio spec for this component is approved and linked
  • [ ] [Any required API or service] is available in staging

docs.title: Acceptance Criteria
docs.text:
Each criterion must be binary: pass or fail. No "as expected" or "works correctly" — state exactly what is expected.

# Criterion Pass condition AC-1 [Component/element] renders when [condition] Visible for [persona]; hidden for [other persona] AC-2 [Action] triggers [outcome] [Specific behavior — e.g., "API call returns 200 within 500ms"] AC-3 Error state displays when [failure condition] Specific error message: "[exact text]" AC-4 Feature is NOT accessible without feature flag enabled Returns 404 / UI element absent when flag is off AC-5 Unit tests cover: happy path, error state, flag-off state Test suite passes with ≥80% coverage on new code

docs.title: Exit Criteria
docs.text:
What must be true for this story to be marked "Done". All must be met.

  • [ ] All acceptance criteria above pass in staging
  • [ ] Unit tests written and passing (happy path + error state + flag-off)
  • [ ] Code reviewed and approved by at least one other engineer
  • [ ] No critical/high-severity bugs open against this story
  • [ ] Feature flag tested: both on and off paths verified
  • [ ] QA sign-off from QA engineer

docs.title: Technical Notes
docs.text: Field Value Feature flag API endpoint(s) affected Components modified State management changes i18n strings needed Yes / No Accessibility requirements

docs.title: Design Reference
docs.text: Field Value Design Studio file [link] Specific frame/screen [frame name or link] Component(s) to use [Dialtone component names — e.g., dt-button, dt-input]

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text: Dependency Type Status Story / API / Service / Design Confirmed / Blocked

docs.title: Story Points
docs.text:
Estimated by Engineering during sprint planning. Leave blank until pointed.

Points: ___

docs.title: Labels
docs.text:
Standard labels, e.g.: FY27Q1-support, design-required, feature-flag-required

Agent validation checklist: - [ ] Story fits in ≤1 engineering sprint (3–5 days max) - [ ] Entry criteria list all blockers (no "implied" dependencies) - [ ] Every acceptance criterion has a specific, measurable pass condition - [ ] Exit criteria include unit tests AND QA sign-off - [ ] Feature flag field present — not guessed, left for Engineering - [ ] Dialtone component names used in design reference (not generic "dropdown" or "button")

docs.title: Product Deep Dive — [Product Area] · [Quarter]
docs.text:
Purpose: Give EPD leadership a clear view of outcomes, what's working/not, and what to focus on. Target: ≤ 4 pages as pre-read · ≤ 30 minutes live · drive decisions, not status theater.

How to use this template: - One master Google Doc per product area. Add a new tab for each quarter / deep dive update. - Top of each tab: "Qx FYyy — Latest Update" section. - Keep one prior update in the same tab; move older updates to an archival tab. - Pre-read: share in PM channel + calendar invite by EOD Friday before the Tuesday meeting. - Owners presenting: PM · Eng Lead · Design Lead. - Optional: link up to 3 slides (visuals only — funnels, timelines, architectures) from the doc. - Generate this doc using the /product-deep-dive skill.

docs.title: Section 1 — Header
docs.text: Field Value Product Area Support / Connect / Sell / Ecosystem / SharedServices Quarter Qx FYyy PM Eng Lead Design Lead Key Links [Adoption dashboard] · [Engagement dashboard] · [Revenue dashboard] Visual slides (optional) [Link — max 3 slides] Prior update [Link to prior tab/section]

docs.title: Section 2 — Executive Summary
docs.text:
Max 8 bullets. All outcome- and decision-focused. A CPO/CTO/HoD reads in 3 minutes and "gets it."

  • Quarter status: Green / Yellow / Red — [1-clause reason]
  • Outcome headline: We [hit/missed] [core outcome] by X% vs target, driven by [Y].
  • What's working (1): [Concrete win tied to a metric or customer impact]
  • What's working (2): [Concrete win tied to a metric or customer impact]
  • What's not working (1): [Concrete issue with scale/impact stated]
  • What's not working (2): [Concrete issue with scale/impact stated]
  • OKR status: [On track / at risk / off — 1-line driver]
  • Forward view: [Next quarter priorities & timeline headline]
  • Yearly theme alignment: "This ladders up to [Theme] via [explanation]."
  • Asks from leadership (max 3): [Framed as "Decide X vs Y" or "Unblock dependency on [team]"]

docs.title: 3.1 Standard Metrics
docs.text:
Leave [DATA NEEDED] if you don't have a value — do not fabricate numbers.

Metric Definition Target (Q) Actual (QTD) Trend vs. last Q Notes Adoption: WAU Adoption: DAU Adoption: % of TAM Engagement: [Core action/WAU] Engagement: Retention / stickiness Revenue: ARR Revenue: NNARR

docs.title: 3.2 Product-Specific Metrics
docs.text:
Top 2–4 metrics that drive the business story for this area.

Metric Definition Target (Q) Actual (QTD) Trend vs. last Q Notes

One sentence of insight per metric — state what it means, not what the number is.

docs.title: Section 4 — What We Shipped & What We Learned
docs.text:
Top 3–5 launches since last update. Focus on learning and impact, not completeness.

Launch: [Name] - What: [One-line description] - Who: [Primary user / segment] - When: [Launch date or window] - Expected outcome: [Metric + direction you were trying to move] - Result: [Early adoption/engagement/revenue data + key qualitative signal] - Insight: "We learned that ___."

(Repeat for each launch)

What's Working (max 3 bullets — patterns of repeatable success tied to metrics)

  • [Pattern] — [Evidence]
  • [Pattern] — [Evidence]
  • [Pattern] — [Evidence]

What's Not Working (max 3 bullets — specific about scale and root cause)

  • [Problem] — [Scale / impact]
  • [Problem] — [Scale / impact]
  • [Problem] — [Scale / impact]

docs.title: NOW — Current quarter (max 3 initiatives)
docs.text:
[Initiative name] - Hypothesis: "We believe [X] will cause [Y] because [Z]." - Primary metric: · Guardrail metric: - Timeline: [Start] → [Key milestones] → [Target customer-facing date] - Status: Green / Yellow / Red — [1-line reason]

(Repeat)

docs.title: NEXT — Next quarter (max 4 initiatives)
docs.text:
[Initiative name] - Problem: [1 sentence] - Primary metric: - Earliest likely start: - Dependencies: [Team + what's needed]

(Repeat)

docs.title: LATER — Following quarters (max 5 — directional only)
docs.text: • [Initiative] — [1 sentence]. Supports Theme: [name].

docs.title: Section 6 — OKR Status Snapshot
docs.text: Objective Key Result Current Goal Status Commentary On track / At risk / Behind / Severely behind

docs.title: Top 3 Risks
docs.text: Risk Probability / Impact Mitigation & Owner Timeline sensitivity H / M / L · H / M / L If not addressed by [date], then [impact]

docs.title: Cross-Team Dependencies
docs.text: Team + Dependency By when Risk if late

docs.title: Explicit Tradeoffs (2–3 bullets)
docs.text: • "We are choosing [X] over [Y] this quarter, which means [Z impact on metric/customer]."

docs.title: Section 8 — GTM, Customer Signal & Adoption Motion
docs.text:
Include only if GTM-driven. Keep short — link to call notes rather than retelling them.

Key customer stories (max 3): [Customer + scenario + what changed: win/loss/churn/expansion]

GTM plays in flight: [Top 2–3 motions tied to this product this quarter]

Adoption blockers from field: [2–3 bullets summarizing what Sales/CS are saying]

docs.title: Section 9 — Asks & Decisions
docs.text:
Max 3 asks. Only include real exec decisions — remove this section if there are none.

# Decision needed Options considered Recommendation Impact of delay Owner / DRI D-1 [Framed as a question] D-2

Pre-read quality check — before sharing: - [ ] Executive Summary readable in ≤ 3 minutes — outcome headline is concrete and honest - [ ] Every metric has a source link or [DATA NEEDED] — no fabricated numbers - [ ] Every Now initiative is timeline-anchored, not just named - [ ] "What's Not Working" is direct — no softening language that hides the real signal - [ ] Tradeoffs are explicit — what are we not doing and what's the cost - [ ] Total length ≤ 4 pages (excluding appendix / slide links) - [ ] Shared in PM channel + calendar invite by EOD Friday

docs.title: UI Templates
docs.text:
Standardized output shapes for Designers. Use as Claude output targets or starting points for new briefs.

  • UI Brief Template

    Full design brief template covering scope, user goals, constraints, success criteria, and Dialtone alignment.

    View template

  • Component Spec Template

    Structured component spec with anatomy, variants, states, Dialtone tokens, and accessibility requirements.

    View template

  • Prototype Brief Template

    Input brief for UI Studio or the Prototype Builder agent — scope, user flows, key screens, and Dialtone component list.

    View template

Using templates with Claude

Paste the template into Claude with the prompt: "Fill in this design brief for [feature]." Or use the UI Brief skill which targets this template automatically.

docs.title: Component Spec Template — [Component Name]
docs.text: Instructions for AI agents: Use the Dialtone MCP (mcp__dialtone__search_components, mcp__dialtone__search_tokens) to verify token names before writing them in. Do not use raw hex values — use --dt- tokens. Do not invent component names that don't exist in Dialtone.

docs.title: Header
docs.text: Field Value Component Name Dialtone base component e.g. DtButton, DtInput, DtModal — or "New" if not extending existing Designer Coding UI Studio link JIRA ticket Status Draft / In Review / Approved

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: What does this component do? Who uses it? Where does it appear in the product?

docs.title: 2. Anatomy
docs.text:
Label each part of the component. Use the UI Studio link above for visual reference.

Part Required? Notes [Part 1 — e.g. Label] Yes [Part 2 — e.g. Icon] No [Part 3 — e.g. Helper text] No Shown only in error state

docs.title: 3. Variants
docs.text: Variant Description When to use Default [Variant 2] [Variant 3]

docs.title: 4. States
docs.text: State Visual description Notes Default Hover Focus Active / Pressed Disabled Loading Error Empty / Zero state

docs.title: 5. UI Tokens
docs.text:
Reference Dialtone tokens using --dt- prefix. Use the Dialtone MCP to confirm exact token names.

Property Token Value (for reference) Background (default) --dt-color-surface-primary Border --dt-color-border-default Text --dt-color-foreground-primary Border radius --dt-radius-300 Spacing (padding) --dt-space-400 Focus ring --dt-color-border-focus

docs.title: 6. Accessibility
docs.text: Requirement Implementation ARIA role Keyboard navigation Focus management Screen reader label Color contrast Minimum 4.5:1 (WCAG AA)

docs.title: 7. Responsive Behavior
docs.text:
How does the component behave at different breakpoints?

Breakpoint Behavior Mobile (<768px) Tablet (768–1024px) Desktop (>1024px)

docs.title: 9. Coding Notes
docs.text: Optional — for complex components, include notes on implementation considerations, API surface, or performance constraints.

docs.title: 10. Open Questions
docs.text: # Question Owner Target resolution Q-1

docs.title: UI Brief Template — [Feature Name]
docs.text: Instructions: Complete this brief before starting design work. It is the PM's handoff to UI. All fields marked (required) must be filled before UI begins. Load 01_context/personas-and-icps/DP-General-user-personas.md before filling in Section 3.

docs.title: Header
docs.text: Field Value Feature Name Core Area Support / Connect / Sell / Ecosystem / SharedServices PM Designer JIRA Epic UI Start Date Target Gate 1

docs.title: 1. Problem Statement (required)
docs.text: In 2–3 sentences: What user problem are we solving? What is the current experience and why does it fall short?

docs.title: 2. Jobs to Be Done (required)
docs.text:
What is the user trying to accomplish? Write in the format: "When [situation], I want to [motivation], so I can [outcome]."

# JTBD Statement 1 When , I want to , so I can ___ 2

docs.title: 3. Target Personas (required)
docs.text:
Who is this for? Reference 01_context/personas-and-icps/DP-General-user-personas.md.

Persona Primary / Secondary Key need in this context

docs.title: 4. Scope (required)
docs.text:
What UI must cover: - Surface(s): [ ] Web app [ ] Mobile [ ] Admin portal [ ] Email/notification [ ] Other: ___ - States: [ ] Empty [ ] Loading [ ] Error [ ] Success [ ] Partial data - Edge cases to design for: - Edge case 1: - Edge case 2:

What is explicitly NOT in scope for this design: - Item 1 - Item 2

docs.title: 5. Business Context (required)
docs.text:
Why does this matter to the business? What FY27 priority does it support?

Business driver:

Success metrics UI should optimize for: | Metric | Current | Target | |---|---|---| | | | |

docs.title: 6. Constraints & Requirements (required)
docs.text: Type Requirement Dialtone Must use DT9 Component Library + DT9 UI Tokens (Rebrand 2025) Accessibility WCAG 2.1 AA minimum — specify if higher standard required Responsive [ ] Desktop only [ ] Tablet [ ] Mobile Dark mode [ ] Required [ ] Not required Localization [ ] English only [ ] Multi-language — specify languages: ___ Coding constraint Any known technical limitations

docs.title: 7. Research & Insights (if available)
docs.text:
Summarize relevant research, NPS themes, support tickets, or customer interviews. Link to full research if available.

Signal type Key finding Source Customer interview Support tickets NPS / CSAT Competitive reference

docs.title: 8. Competitive Reference (if applicable)
docs.text:
How do key competitors approach this? What should we learn from or avoid?

Competitor Their approach Notes

docs.title: 9. Prior Art & Related Work (if applicable)
docs.text:
Existing Dialpad screens, components, or patterns that are relevant or should be reused.

  • UI Studio reference: [link]
  • Related feature: [JIRA / Confluence link]

docs.title: 10. Timeline & Milestones
docs.text: Milestone Target Date UI exploration complete UI review with UI Lead Gate 1 deck ready Gate 1 with EPD Staff UI handoff to Coding

docs.title: 11. Open Questions for UI
docs.text:
Questions that UI should answer through exploration or research. PM will answer any product/business questions before UI starts.

# Question Who answers D-1 UI P-1 PM

PM checklist before handing to UI: - [ ] JTBD and problem statement complete - [ ] Personas confirmed and in scope - [ ] Scope (in/out) explicitly defined - [ ] Business metrics defined with baselines - [ ] All product-level open questions answered - [ ] JIRA epic created

docs.title: Prototype Brief Template — [Feature Name]
docs.text: Instructions for AI agents: Use this brief as input to UI Studio or the Prototype Builder agent. Fill in all sections before generating. The "User Flows" section is the most critical — be specific about what the user sees and does at each step. Use Dialtone component names where possible.

docs.title: Header
docs.text: Field Value Feature Name Core Area Support / Connect / Sell / Ecosystem / SharedServices PM Designer PRD link Prototype tool UI Studio / Claude Code (React) / Other Target Gate Gate 1 / Gate 2 / Internal review

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text: What decisions does this prototype need to help make? What questions does it need to answer? (Max 3 bullet points.)

docs.title: Out of scope (static / not needed):
docs.text: Fidelity level: Low / Mid / High Low = wireframe blocks; Mid = Dialtone components, no real data; High = real or realistic data, polished UI

docs.title: 3. User Flows
docs.text: For each key flow, describe what the user sees and does. Be specific — this is what UI Studio and the Prototype Builder agent use to generate screens.

docs.title: Flow 1: [Flow Name]
docs.text:
Entry point: Where does the user start?

  1. User sees:
  2. User does:
  3. System shows:
  4. User sees:
  5. User does:

Success state: What does "done" look like for this flow?

Error / edge state: What happens if something goes wrong?

docs.title: Flow 2: [Flow Name]
docs.text: (repeat structure above)

docs.title: 4. Key Screens
docs.text:
List the screens that must exist in the prototype.

Screen Description Primary action [Screen 1] [Screen 2]

docs.title: 5. Dialtone Components to Use
docs.text:
List the Dialtone components this prototype should use. Use the Dialtone MCP to confirm component names.

Component Dialtone name Usage in prototype

docs.title: 6. Content / Copy Notes
docs.text: Are there specific strings, labels, or empty states that must appear exactly as specified? List them here.

docs.title: 7. What Reviewers Will Test
docs.text:
What specific questions will Gate reviewers or user research participants be asked to answer using this prototype?

  1. Can participants find [X] without prompting?
  2. Does [Y] flow feel intuitive or does it cause confusion at [step]?

docs.title: Coding Skills
docs.text:
• /postmortem-draft — Draft a Blameless Postmortem from Incident Data

    Drafts a blameless postmortem document from incident timeline, alert data, and remediation notes. Structures the narrative, surfaces contributing factors, and generates action items with owners. Output is ready for async review and Confluence publish.

    View skill

  • /rfc-scaffold — Scaffold an RFC or Coding Spec from a Problem Statement

    Generates a structured RFC or Coding Spec from a problem statement, producing all required sections with targeted questions where information is missing. Follows the format used across Dialpad RFCs. Output is ready to paste into Confluence for review.

    View skill

  • /spec-to-code-context — Generate In-Code Context from a Spec

    Converts a PRD section or feature spec into in-code context comments (TSDoc/JSDoc) that engineers and downstream agents can use to understand the business intent of a module without reading the full PRD. This is the primary handoff mechanism from PM/UI spec to Coding agent workflows.

    View skill

  • /test-from-spec — Generate Tests from a Spec or Existing Code

    Generates Vitest (Vue 3) or pytest (Python) tests from a spec or existing code. Covers happy path, error states, and feature flag behavior. Output feeds directly into the engineering review workflow.

    View skill

  • /ticket-breakdown — Break a JIRA Epic into Scoped, Agent-Sized Stories

    Takes a JIRA epic (or epic description) and breaks it into stories that are correctly scoped for individual engineer sessions. Coding-focused — considers implementation complexity, not just product scope.
