docs.title: Design Studio MCP Setup Guide
docs.text:
This guide has been merged into the Figma MCP Setup Guide.

-> Go to Figma MCP Setup Guide

The Figma MCP now uses a remote OAuth server — no personal access token needed. Run:

claude mcp add --transport http figma https://mcp.figma.com/mcp

See the Figma guide for full details.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Configures the Dialtone MCP for live design-system lookups — tokens, icons, components, and usage guidelines — directly from Claude Code sessions. Useful for design critique, component generation, and ensuring code matches the design system.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Claude Code installed

No authentication is required — the Dialtone MCP serves public design system documentation.

docs.title: Step 1: Add MCP Configuration
docs.text:
Add this config to your ~/.mcp.json file inside "mcpServers":

"dialtone": {
  "command": "npx",
  "args": ["-y", "@dialpad/dialtone-mcp-server"]
}

No API token or env vars needed.

docs.title: Step 2: Verify the Connection
docs.text:
/mcp list

dialtone should appear. Then test:

What color tokens are available in Dialtone for status indicators?

docs.title: Look up a component
docs.text: Show me the props and usage guidelines for the DtButton component.

docs.title: Check available icons
docs.text: List all icons in Dialtone that relate to "phone" or "call".

docs.title: Validate token usage
docs.text: What is the correct Dialtone spacing token for 16px gaps between elements?

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Issue Solution MCP not listed Restart Claude Code after editing config Stale data Run npx @dialpad/dialtone-mcp-server@latest to force a fresh install

docs.title: Known Limitations
docs.text:
• Read-only — cannot modify design system tokens or components
  • Component documentation quality depends on upstream Dialtone docs

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Configures the Figma MCP server so that Claude Code can read Figma file content, extract component data, and reference designs during code generation and design critique sessions. Required for /design-critique, /component-spec, and /spec-to-code-context to work with live Figma designs.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Claude Code installed
  • Figma account with access to Dialpad's Figma organization

docs.title: Step 1: Add the Figma MCP
docs.text:
Run this command in your terminal:

claude mcp add --transport http figma https://mcp.figma.com/mcp

docs.title: Step 2: Authenticate
docs.text: On first use, Claude Code will open your browser for Figma OAuth. Log in with your Figma account that has access to the Dialpad organization. No personal access token to create or manage.

docs.title: Step 3: Verify the Connection
docs.text:
Start a Claude Code session:

/mcp list

figma should appear. Then test with a real Figma file URL:

Read the frame "Agent Dashboard" from this Figma file: [paste Figma URL]

docs.title: Libraries (enable in all Figma files)
docs.text: Library Contents DT9 Component Library Rebrand 2025 All Dialtone Vue components (58+) DT9 Design Tokens Color, typography, spacing, shadow tokens DT9 Icon Library Dialpad icon set

docs.title: Key Design Files
docs.text:
File Link Contents Dialtone Component Specs (internal Figma org) Component usage documentation Dialpad Design System (internal Figma org) Brand and product design guidelines Product Area Files (per Core Area folder) Feature design files

To find the correct Figma URL for your Core Area: search the Dialpad Figma organization by Core Area name (e.g., "Sell", "Connect").

docs.title: Extract component spec from a Figma frame
docs.text:
Read the "CSAT Score Card" frame from [Figma URL] and generate a component spec using
the template in 01_context/design-system/DP-General-component-library.md

docs.title: Critique a design against Dialtone
docs.text:
Read the "Power Dialer" screen from [Figma URL].
Compare it against Dialtone DT9 standards from 01_context/design-system/DP-General-tokens.md
and flag any violations.

docs.title: Check design for accessibility
docs.text:
Read all interactive elements in [Figma URL] and list any that are missing aria labels
or have contrast ratios below 4.5:1.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Issue Solution "Could not access file" Check that your Figma account has view access to the file OAuth expired Re-authenticate via browser — re-run the claude mcp add command MCP not listed Restart Claude Code after adding the MCP Frame not found Use the exact frame name as it appears in Figma layers panel

docs.title: Known Limitations
docs.text:
• Read-only — cannot create or edit Figma designs via MCP
  • Cannot extract interactive prototyping flows — only static frame content
  • Large files (>500 frames) may timeout — specify the exact frame name to narrow scope
  • Auto Layout details may not fully translate to component spec — Designer review required

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Configures the GitHub MCP server so that Claude Code can read PR diffs, check CI status, list issues, and post code review comments directly from agent sessions. Required for the 04_agents/engineering/DP-General-code-review/ workflow.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Claude Code installed
  • GitHub account with access to Dialpad's GitHub organization (github.com/dialpad)

docs.title: Step 1: Add the GitHub MCP
docs.text:
Run this command in your terminal:

claude mcp add --transport http github https://api.githubcopilot.com/mcp/

docs.title: Step 2: Authenticate
docs.text: On first use, Claude Code will open your browser for GitHub OAuth. Authorize the app with your GitHub account that has access to the dialpad org. No Personal Access Token to create or manage.

docs.title: Step 3: Verify the Connection
docs.text:
Start a Claude Code session and run:

/mcp list

You should see github listed. Then test:

List the open PRs in dialpad/web-clients assigned to me.

docs.title: Step 4: Key Dialpad Repositories
docs.text: Repository Contents Common use dialpad/web-clients Dialpad 2.0 frontend (Vue 3) Code review, component generation dialpad/firespotter Dialpad 1.0 frontend + Python backend Legacy code context dialpad/dialtone Dialtone design system monorepo Component library reference dialpad/ubervoice Core telephony backend Backend service context

docs.title: Read a PR diff for code review
docs.text:
Read the diff for PR #4521 in dialpad/web-clients and run a code review
using the standards in 01_context/codebase-map/DP-General-key-patterns.md

docs.title: Post a review comment
docs.text:
Post the following review comment on line 42 of src/components/CsatCard.vue in PR #4521:
[paste comment]

docs.title: Check CI status
docs.text: What is the CI status for PR #4521 in dialpad/web-clients?

docs.title: List open issues for triage
docs.text: List open issues labeled "on-call" in dialpad/web-clients created in the last 24 hours.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Issue Solution "Not authorized" Re-authenticate via browser — the OAuth grant may have expired Cannot see Dialpad repos Confirm you've been granted org access via Okta → GitHub app MCP not listed Restart Claude Code after adding the MCP

docs.title: Alternative: Use the gh CLI Directly
docs.text:
For most day-to-day GitHub operations, the GitHub CLI (gh) is a lighter-weight alternative that requires no MCP configuration. Claude Code can run gh commands natively in the terminal — just authenticate once with gh auth login. This covers PRs, issues, CI checks, code search, and more without managing PATs or MCP config files. See the full comparison and examples in Claude Code Best Practices.

The MCP remains useful when you need structured JSON responses for programmatic processing or when skills/agents are already wired to use MCP tools.

docs.title: Known Limitations
docs.text:
• Cannot approve or merge PRs directly — requires human approval per Dialpad's branch protection rules
  • Cannot run CI pipelines — only read status
  • Rate limited to 5,000 API requests/hour
  • Does not access private Design Studio links in PR descriptions

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Configures the Glean MCP so Claude Code can search Dialpad's internal knowledge base — Confluence docs, past PRDs, research summaries, customer data, and competitive intel — directly from agent sessions. Already pre-configured for this repository via the Glean Claude Code integration.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Claude Code installed
  • Dialpad Glean account (all Dialpad employees have access via SSO)

docs.title: Step 1: Add MCP Configuration
docs.text:
Add this config to your ~/.mcp.json file inside "mcpServers":

"glean": {
  "command": "npx",
  "args": ["-y", "@gleanwork/local-mcp-server"],
  "env": {
    "GLEAN_SERVER_URL": "https://dialpad-be.glean.com"
  }
}

The Glean MCP uses OAuth 2.1 by default — on first use it will open your browser for Okta SSO authentication. No API token to create or manage.

Note: The Glean MCP is already active in this repository via the mcp__glean_claude-code integration. If you are working within DPAIPlaybook, you can use Glean search without additional setup.

docs.title: Step 2: Verify the Connection
docs.text:
/mcp list

glean or glean_claude-code should appear. Then test:

Search Glean for recent PRDs about AI CSAT in the Support core area.

docs.title: Glean Search Best Practices
docs.text: Getting the most out of Glean — whether through the web UI, Slack, or the Claude Code MCP — comes down to knowing the right search techniques. These tips are sourced from the official Glean docs.

docs.title: Search Operators
docs.text: Operator What it does Example "term" Requires the exact word in results "featureY" "exact phrase" Requires words appear adjacent, in order "coaching summary" "word1" "word2" Requires both words anywhere in doc "CSAT" "Support" updated: Filters by modification date updated:yesterday, updated:past_week, updated:past_month from: Filters by person (owner, contributor, assignee, commenter) from:jane.doe, from:me type: Filters by document type type:presentation, type:bug app: Filters by source application app:confluence, app:jira, app:google-drive my:history Restricts to docs you've visited in the last 6 months my:history power dialer

docs.title: Tips for Better Results
docs.text:
• Use natural language or keywords — Glean supports both. "How does billing handle refunds?" and billing refund process both work.
  • Search content, not just titles — Glean indexes full document content, so you can find docs even if you don't know the title.
  • Append file types — Add pptx, pdf, or doc to filter by format (e.g., competitive analysis pptx).
  • Use autocomplete — Start typing a document title you've visited before and Glean will suggest it.
  • Combine operators — Stack filters for precision: from:me updated:past_week type:presentation.

docs.title: Advanced Features
docs.text:
• Custom field filters — For complex apps like JIRA, Salesforce, or Zendesk, you can filter by custom fields. Click the info icon in the search box to see available fields for the current result set.
  • App exclusion — Use the sidebar filter toggles to hide irrelevant apps from results.
  • Zoom transcripts — Meeting recordings and their transcripts surface automatically when relevant.
  • Advanced query example: status:"closed won" vertical:"enterprise" feature name

docs.title: Glean in Slack
docs.text: Use the /glean command in any Slack channel to search inline — results are visible only to you unless you share them. This is handy for quick lookups during conversations.

docs.title: Browser Extension Shortcuts
docs.text: Platform Shortcut Mac Cmd+J opens the Glean sidebar Windows Alt+J opens the Glean sidebar Firefox Control+J opens the Glean sidebar

docs.title: Sharing Searches
docs.text: You can copy a Glean search URL and share it with teammates. Results are always personalized and permission-filtered per viewer — the same link may return different results depending on what each person has access to.

docs.title: Find prior art before writing a PRD
docs.text:
Search Glean for any PRDs or specs related to "power dialer auto-advance" in Sell.
Include documents from the last 2 years.

docs.title: Pull competitive intel
docs.text: Search Glean for the latest Genesys competitive analysis or battle card.

docs.title: Find a person or team
docs.text: Who owns the billing infrastructure at Dialpad? Who is the PM for SharedServices?

docs.title: Check past decisions
docs.text: Search Glean for the decision log or ADR for how Dialpad handles feature flag rollouts.

docs.title: What Glean Indexes at Dialpad
docs.text: Source Contents Confluence PDLC docs, engineering ADRs, team wikis, meeting notes Google Drive PRDs, design briefs, research decks, OKR docs JIRA Epics, stories, bug reports (searchable by label/project) Slack Public channel history (select channels) Highspot Sales enablement, competitive intel, pricing guides GitHub PR descriptions and README files

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Issue Solution Authentication failed Re-authenticate via browser — restart Claude Code to trigger OAuth flow "No results" Try broader search terms; Glean uses semantic search Can't find a specific doc Use from:name or updated:past_month filters

docs.title: Known Limitations
docs.text:
• Results are permission-filtered — if you cannot access a doc in Glean's UI, the MCP will not return it
  • Indexing is not real-time — newly created docs may take 24-48 hours to appear
  • Cannot create or edit documents via the Glean MCP

docs.title: Important Notice
docs.text:
There is no official @modelcontextprotocol/server-gmail package — the package name previously documented here does not exist on npm.

For Gmail integration with Claude Code, use one of these community alternatives:

Package What it covers Install google-workspace-mcp Gmail + Calendar + Drive npx google-workspace-mcp mcp-gsuite (by MarkusPfundstein) Gmail + Calendar + Drive npx mcp-gsuite

Both require a Google Cloud OAuth 2.0 client and a one-time browser authentication flow.

docs.title: Setup (using google-workspace-mcp)
docs.text:
1. Create a Google Cloud project and enable the Gmail API
  2. Create an OAuth 2.0 Client ID (Desktop App type)
  3. Download the client credentials JSON file
  4. Add to your ~/.mcp.json:
"gmail": {
  "command": "npx",
  "args": ["-y", "google-workspace-mcp"],
  "env": {
    "GOOGLE_OAUTH_CREDENTIALS": "/path/to/client-credentials.json"
  }
}
  1. On first use, the MCP will open your browser for Google OAuth consent

Refer to the package's README for the latest configuration details.

docs.title: What it enables
docs.text:
• Email search and summarization
  • Draft generation
  • Thread reading and context extraction

docs.title: Known Limitations
docs.text:
• Community-maintained — not officially supported by Google or Anthropic
  • Requires a Google Cloud project with OAuth configured
  • Permission scopes must be carefully reviewed before authorizing

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Configures the Google BigQuery MCP so Claude Code can run SQL queries against Dialpad's analytics data warehouse directly from agent sessions. Enables data-grounded PRD writing, success metric baselining, and on-call log analysis without needing a separate BI tool.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Claude Code installed
  • Access to Dialpad's BigQuery project (request via IT or your Analytics team)
  • IAM roles: BigQuery Job User and BigQuery Data Viewer (request via IT if you don't have them)

docs.title: Step 1: Add the BigQuery MCP
docs.text:
Run this command in your terminal:

claude mcp add --transport http google-bigquery https://bigquery.googleapis.com/mcp

docs.title: Step 2: Authenticate
docs.text: On first use, Claude Code will open your browser for Google OAuth. Log in with your @dialpad.com Google Workspace account. The MCP uses your existing IAM permissions — no service account key to manage.

docs.title: Step 3: Verify the Connection
docs.text:
/mcp list

google-bigquery should appear. Then test:

List the datasets available in the dialpad-analytics BigQuery project.

docs.title: Dialpad BigQuery Key Datasets
docs.text:
Contact your Analytics team for the current dataset names. Common areas:

Dataset area What it contains Call data Call records, duration, outcome, MOS scores User activity Feature usage events, login activity AI features Transcription accuracy, CSAT scores, coaching data Billing Subscription data, plan changes, revenue metrics Product analytics Funnel data, feature adoption, retention cohorts

Always query staging/non-PII datasets for development work. Production datasets with PII require additional data governance approval.

docs.title: Baseline a success metric before a feature launch
docs.text:
Write a BigQuery SQL query to find the average number of manual clicks
between calls in the Power Dialer flow, grouped by week, for the last 90 days.
Dataset: [ask Analytics team for the correct dataset name]

docs.title: Pull call quality distribution for on-call triage
docs.text:
Query BigQuery for the distribution of MOS scores in the last 24 hours.
Flag any regions with MOS < 3.5.

docs.title: Validate feature flag adoption
docs.text:
How many unique orgs have had the dp_coaching_csat_v2 feature flag enabled
in the last 30 days? Break down by plan tier.

docs.title: Data Governance Rules
docs.text:
• Do not query tables containing customer PII without explicit approval from Legal/Privacy
  • Do not export raw query results to shared documents — aggregate before sharing
  • Use parameterized queries when possible to avoid SQL injection
  • Always include date-range filters to avoid scanning entire tables (cost control)

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Issue Solution "Permission denied" Verify you have BigQuery Job User and Data Viewer IAM roles — request via IT "Project not found" Use dialpad-analytics as the project ID OAuth expired Re-authenticate via browser — restart Claude Code to trigger OAuth flow MCP not listed Restart Claude Code after adding the MCP

docs.title: Known Limitations
docs.text:
• Read-only — cannot create tables or modify data via MCP
  • Complex queries may timeout — add date-range filters and LIMIT clauses
  • Service does not support all BigQuery features (e.g., DML, DDL operations)
  • Query costs are tracked per user — be mindful of full-table scans

docs.title: Important Notice
docs.text:
There is no official @modelcontextprotocol/server-google-calendar package — the package name previously documented here does not exist on npm.

For Google Calendar integration with Claude Code, use one of these community alternatives:

Package What it covers Install google-workspace-mcp Gmail + Calendar + Drive npx google-workspace-mcp mcp-gsuite (by MarkusPfundstein) Gmail + Calendar + Drive npx mcp-gsuite

Both require a Google Cloud OAuth 2.0 client and a one-time browser authentication flow.

docs.title: Setup (using google-workspace-mcp)
docs.text:
1. Create a Google Cloud project and enable the Google Calendar API
  2. Create an OAuth 2.0 Client ID (Desktop App type)
  3. Download the client credentials JSON file
  4. Add to your ~/.mcp.json:
"google-calendar": {
  "command": "npx",
  "args": ["-y", "google-workspace-mcp"],
  "env": {
    "GOOGLE_OAUTH_CREDENTIALS": "/path/to/client-credentials.json"
  }
}
  1. On first use, the MCP will open your browser for Google OAuth consent

Refer to the package's README for the latest configuration details.

docs.title: What it enables
docs.text:
• Meeting context and scheduling awareness
  • Agenda drafting
  • Calendar search (find meetings by topic, attendee, or date)

docs.title: Known Limitations
docs.text:
• Community-maintained — not officially supported by Google or Anthropic
  • Requires a Google Cloud project with OAuth configured
  • Cannot create or modify calendar events via some packages — check the README

docs.title: Deprecation Notice
docs.text:
The official @modelcontextprotocol/server-gdrive package exists on npm but is deprecated and archived. It still works but will not receive updates.

For a maintained alternative, consider google-workspace-mcp or mcp-gsuite which cover Drive, Gmail, and Calendar in a single package.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Claude Code installed
  • A Google Cloud project with the Drive API enabled
  • An OAuth 2.0 Client ID (Desktop App type)

docs.title: Step 1: Create Google Cloud OAuth Credentials
docs.text:
1. Go to Google Cloud Console
  2. Create a new project (or use an existing one)
  3. Enable the Google Drive API
  4. Go to Credentials -> Create Credentials -> OAuth Client ID
  5. Application type: Desktop App
  6. Download the credentials JSON file
  7. Rename it to gcp-oauth.keys.json and place it in the server's working directory

docs.title: Step 2: Add MCP Configuration
docs.text:
Add this config to your ~/.mcp.json file inside "mcpServers":

"google-drive": {
  "command": "npx",
  "args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-gdrive"]
}

The server looks for gcp-oauth.keys.json in its working directory. On first run, it will open your browser for Google OAuth consent and save credentials to .gdrive-server-credentials.json.

docs.title: Step 3: Verify the Connection
docs.text:
/mcp list

google-drive should appear. Then test:

Search Google Drive for documents containing "Q2 OKR" updated in the last month.

docs.title: What it enables
docs.text:
• Read and reference Google Docs, Sheets, and Slides
  • Search Drive by title, content, or date
  • Use documents as context for Claude Code sessions

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Issue Solution OAuth flow fails Ensure the Drive API is enabled in your Google Cloud project "Access denied" Re-run the OAuth flow — credentials may have expired MCP not listed Restart Claude Code after editing config

docs.title: Known Limitations
docs.text:
• Package is deprecated — may stop working in the future
  • Read-only — cannot create or edit Drive files via MCP
  • Requires a Google Cloud project with OAuth configured
  • Large files may be truncated

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Before adopting or recommending any self-hosted MCP server that requires API keys, tokens, or stored credentials, the recommendation must be cleared with the Security team.

This policy applies to any MCP server where:

  • You must generate and store an API key or token locally (e.g., in environment variables or config files)
  • The server runs as a local process on your machine via npx, uvx, or similar
  • The server is a community/third-party package (not an official vendor-hosted remote MCP)

docs.title: Why This Matters
docs.text:
Self-hosted MCP servers that require API keys introduce risks that vendor-hosted OAuth servers do not:

  • Credential exposure — API keys stored in config files (~/.mcp.json, .env) can be accidentally committed, leaked, or accessed by other processes
  • Supply chain risk — Community npm/PyPI packages may not have the same security review as vendor-hosted services
  • Scope creep — API keys may grant broader permissions than the MCP server actually needs
  • No centralized revocation — Unlike OAuth tokens managed by IT, personal API keys are harder to audit and revoke

docs.title: What Requires Clearance
docs.text: Requires Security clearance Does NOT require clearance Community npm packages (e.g., google-workspace-mcp, mcp-gsuite) Vendor-hosted remote OAuth MCPs (Atlassian, GitHub, Figma, Amplitude, BigQuery, Monday.com) Any MCP needing a GOOGLE_OAUTH_CREDENTIALS file or similar MCPs with no authentication (Dialtone, Playwright) Self-hosted servers requiring API tokens in env vars MCPs where you authenticate via browser and no keys are stored locally Deprecated/archived official packages (e.g., @modelcontextprotocol/server-gdrive)

docs.title: Current Status of MCP Guides
docs.text:
The following guides in this playbook recommend servers that fall under this policy:

Guide Package(s) Status Gmail google-workspace-mcp, mcp-gsuite Pending Security review Google Calendar google-workspace-mcp, mcp-gsuite Pending Security review Google Drive @modelcontextprotocol/server-gdrive (deprecated) Pending Security review

All other MCP guides in this playbook use vendor-hosted remote OAuth or require no authentication.

docs.title: How to Request Clearance for a New MCP
docs.text:
Before adding a new MCP recommendation to this playbook:

  1. Identify the MCP server's GitHub repository — include the link in your request
  2. Review the MCP's GitHub page for:
    • Open issues and security-related discussions
    • Maintenance activity (last commit date, release cadence)
    • Dependency footprint and known vulnerabilities
    • Permissions/scopes the server requests
  3. Submit a Security review request including:
    • MCP server name and GitHub repo URL
    • What credentials/keys are required
    • What scopes/permissions the server needs
    • The business use case (why this MCP is needed)
    • Whether there is a vendor-hosted OAuth alternative
  4. Wait for Security approval before adding the guide to this playbook

docs.title: Preferred: Vendor-Hosted Remote OAuth
docs.text:
Whenever possible, prefer MCP servers that use remote OAuth authentication via claude mcp add --transport http. These servers:

  • Are hosted and maintained by the vendor
  • Authenticate via browser (no keys stored locally)
  • Have tokens managed centrally and revocable by IT
  • Are listed as OAuth — no tokens needed in our MCP index

docs.title: Questions?
docs.text: Reach out in #ai-coding on Slack or contact it@dialpad.com for Security review requests.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Configures the Monday.com MCP so Claude Code can read boards, update items, and pull status summaries from Monday.com directly in agent sessions. Used for project tracking and cross-functional status updates.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Claude Code installed
  • Monday.com account with access to Dialpad's Monday workspace

docs.title: Step 1: Find Your Monday.com User ID
docs.text:
You need your numeric user ID for the --scope flag:

  1. Log into monday.com
  2. Click your Avatar (bottom-left) → My profile
  3. Look at the URL in your browser — it will look like: 
    https://dialpad.monday.com/users/12345678
  4. Copy the number at the end (e.g. 12345678) — that's your user ID

docs.title: Step 2: Add the Monday.com MCP
docs.text:
Run this command in your terminal, replacing USER_ID with the number from Step 1:

claude mcp add monday --transport http https://mcp.monday.com/mcp --scope user:USER_ID

For example, if your user ID is 12345678:

claude mcp add monday --transport http https://mcp.monday.com/mcp --scope user:12345678

docs.title: Step 3: Authenticate
docs.text: On first use, Claude Code will open your browser for Monday.com OAuth. Authorize the app with your Monday.com account that has access to the Dialpad workspace. No API token to create or manage — authentication is handled automatically.

docs.title: Step 4: Verify the Connection
docs.text:
Start a Claude Code session and run:

/mcp list

monday should appear. Then test:

List the boards in my Monday.com workspace.

docs.title: Get project status
docs.text: Summarize the status of all items on the "Q2 Launch Tracker" board.

docs.title: Update an item
docs.text: Mark item "CSAT Dashboard v2" as "Done" on the Product Launches board.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Issue Solution "Authentication failed" Re-authenticate — run claude mcp add monday --transport http https://mcp.monday.com/mcp --scope user:YOUR_ID again Board not found Check the exact board name in Monday.com MCP not listed Restart Claude Code after adding the MCP Wrong scope error Verify your user ID from the Monday.com profile URL

docs.title: Known Limitations
docs.text:
• Cannot create new boards via MCP — only read and update existing ones
  • Rate limited by Monday.com API quotas

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Configures the Playwright MCP server so that Claude Code can control a browser — navigate pages, click elements, fill forms, take screenshots, and inspect page content. This is useful for end-to-end testing, UI verification, web scraping, and debugging frontend issues directly from the CLI. The Playwright MCP uses accessibility snapshots by default, providing a structured view of page content without requiring screenshots for most interactions.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Claude Code installed
  • Node.js 18+ installed (node --version to check)

docs.title: Step 1: Add the Playwright MCP
docs.text:
Run this command in your terminal:

claude mcp add playwright --launch "npx @playwright/mcp@latest"

This tells Claude Code to launch the Playwright MCP server as a local process using npx. No API keys or authentication required — it runs entirely on your machine.

docs.title: Step 2: Verify the Connection
docs.text:
Start a new Claude Code session:

/mcp list

playwright should appear in the list. Then test with a quick browser action:

Navigate to https://dialpad.com and take a snapshot of the page

Claude should open a browser, navigate to the page, and return a structured snapshot of the page content.

docs.title: Headless vs. Headed Mode
docs.text:
By default, Playwright MCP runs in headed mode (you can see the browser window). To run headless (no visible browser):

claude mcp add playwright --launch "npx @playwright/mcp@latest --headless"

docs.title: Vision Mode
docs.text:
If you want Claude to use screenshots instead of accessibility snapshots (useful for visual verification):

claude mcp add playwright --launch "npx @playwright/mcp@latest --vision"

docs.title: Custom Browser
docs.text:
By default it uses Chromium. To use a different browser:

# Use Firefox
claude mcp add playwright --launch "npx @playwright/mcp@latest --browser firefox"

# Use WebKit (Safari engine)
claude mcp add playwright --launch "npx @playwright/mcp@latest --browser webkit"

docs.title: Alternative: Configure via ~/.mcp.json
docs.text:
Instead of the CLI command, you can add Playwright to your ~/.mcp.json:

{
  "mcpServers": {
    "playwright": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["@playwright/mcp@latest"]
    }
  }
}

With options:

{
  "mcpServers": {
    "playwright": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["@playwright/mcp@latest", "--headless", "--vision"]
    }
  }
}

docs.title: Navigate and inspect a page
docs.text: Go to https://app.dialpad.com/settings and take a snapshot — what elements are visible?

docs.title: Fill out and submit a form
docs.text: Navigate to [URL], fill in the "Email" field with test@example.com, then click the "Submit" button

docs.title: Take a screenshot for visual review
docs.text: Navigate to [URL] and take a screenshot of the current page

docs.title: Test a user flow end-to-end
docs.text:
Go to [staging URL], log in with test credentials, navigate to the contacts page,
and verify that the search bar is present and functional

docs.title: Debug a UI issue
docs.text:
Navigate to [URL], open the browser console, and check for any JavaScript errors.
Then take a snapshot of the page and describe the layout.

docs.title: Extract data from a web page
docs.text: Go to [URL] and extract all the pricing plan names and their monthly costs into a table

docs.title: Available Tools
docs.text:
Once connected, Claude Code has access to these Playwright capabilities:

Tool What it does browser_navigate Go to a URL browser_click Click an element on the page browser_fill_form Type text into input fields browser_snapshot Get an accessibility snapshot of the page (default mode) browser_take_screenshot Capture a screenshot of the page browser_press_key Press keyboard keys (Enter, Tab, etc.) browser_select_option Select from dropdown menus browser_hover Hover over an element browser_drag Drag and drop elements browser_file_upload Upload files to file inputs browser_evaluate Run JavaScript in the browser console browser_console_messages Read browser console output browser_network_requests Inspect network requests browser_navigate_back Go back to previous page browser_tabs List and switch between browser tabs browser_resize Resize the browser window browser_handle_dialog Accept or dismiss dialogs (alerts, confirms) browser_wait_for Wait for elements or conditions browser_close Close the browser

docs.title: QA Verification on Staging
docs.text:
Navigate to https://staging.dialpad.com, log in, go to the Contact Center page,
and verify that the real-time dashboard widgets are rendering correctly

docs.title: Design Review Against Live UI
docs.text:
Navigate to [staging URL for feature X] and take a screenshot.
Compare the layout against the Figma spec at [Figma URL] — flag any visual differences.

docs.title: Accessibility Audit
docs.text:
Navigate to [URL] and take a snapshot. Check all interactive elements for proper
ARIA labels, roles, and keyboard navigability. List any WCAG 2.1 AA violations.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Issue Solution npx: command not found Install Node.js 18+ — brew install node on macOS Browser fails to launch Ensure Chromium dependencies are installed: npx playwright install chromium MCP not listed after adding Restart Claude Code session Page times out Check VPN connection; some internal apps require VPN "Target closed" error The browser was closed unexpectedly — start a new interaction Slow performance Use --headless mode for faster execution Cannot interact with element Use browser_snapshot first to find the correct element reference

docs.title: Known Limitations
docs.text:
• Runs locally on your machine — browser actions are visible in headed mode (use --headless to avoid)
  • Cannot persist browser sessions across Claude Code conversations — each session starts fresh
  • Sites with aggressive bot detection (CAPTCHAs, Cloudflare challenges) may block automated browsing
  • File downloads land in the system's default download directory, not a configurable path
  • Maximum one browser instance at a time per MCP server
  • Cookie/session state is not preserved between separate Claude Code sessions

docs.title: Hooks
docs.text:
Claude Code hooks are shell commands that fire automatically in response to agent events — before or after a tool call, on notification, or when a session ends. They let you automate repetitive actions that happen around the agent's work.

What is a hook?

A hook is a shell command (or script path) configured in ~/.claude/settings.json or .claude/settings.json. When the trigger event fires, Claude Code executes the command and can optionally receive its stdout as additional context.

docs.title: Hook event types
docs.text: Event Fires when Common use cases PreToolUse Before a tool call executes Block dangerous operations, inject context PostToolUse After a tool call completes Lint files after edits, archive artifacts Notification When Claude needs attention Desktop alerts, Slack pings Stop When the agent session ends Summarize + post to Slack, log completion

docs.title: Configure a hook
docs.text:
Add hooks to ~/.claude/settings.json (global) or .claude/settings.json (project-local):

{
  "hooks": {
    "PostToolUse": [
      {
        "matcher": "Write|Edit",
        "hooks": [
          {
            "type": "command",
            "command": "bash ~/.claude/hooks/lint-on-edit.sh"
          }
        ]
      }
    ]
  }
}

The matcher is a regex tested against the tool name (e.g. Write, Edit, Bash).

docs.title: Available hooks at Dialpad
docs.text:
• PM Hooks

    Auto-save PRD artifacts and push JIRA tickets from agent output — zero copy-paste.

    PM hooks

  • Engineering Hooks

    Run linter and tests after every file edit. Feed results back to Claude Code automatically.

    Engineering hooks

  • Design Hooks

    Trigger Design Studio exports and archive design specs when Claude Code writes a component spec.

    Design hooks

  • Cross-functional Hooks

    Notify Slack when an agent session completes with a summary of what was produced.

    Cross-functional hooks

Project-local vs global hooks

Place hooks in .claude/settings.json in the repo root for team-shared behavior. Use ~/.claude/settings.json for personal automation you don't want committed.

docs.title: Coding Hooks
docs.text:
• Lint on Edit GA

    View hook

  • Test Feedback Loop GA

    View hook

docs.title: Lint on Edit
docs.text: Runs ESLint automatically after every Write or Edit tool call on .vue, .ts, or .js files. Lint results are printed to stdout and Claude Code receives them as context — allowing it to self-correct lint errors before you see them.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text:
1. Fires after every Write or Edit tool call
  2. Checks if the modified file ends in .vue, .ts, .js, or .tsx
  3. Runs eslint --fix-dry-run on the file
  4. Prints any errors/warnings to stdout
  5. Claude Code reads the output and can automatically fix errors in the next step

docs.title: Hook script
docs.text:
Save to ~/.claude/hooks/DP-General-lint-on-edit.sh:

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# DP-General-lint-on-edit.sh
# PostToolUse hook: run ESLint after file edits and feed results to Claude Code

set -euo pipefail

INPUT=$(cat)
TOOL=$(echo "$INPUT" | python3 -c "import sys,json; d=json.load(sys.stdin); print(d.get('tool_name',''))")
FILE=$(echo "$INPUT" | python3 -c "import sys,json; d=json.load(sys.stdin); print(d.get('tool_input',{}).get('file_path',''))" 2>/dev/null || echo "")

# Only act on Write/Edit
[[ "$TOOL" == "Write" || "$TOOL" == "Edit" ]] || exit 0

# Only lint JS/TS/Vue files
echo "$FILE" | grep -qE '\.(vue|ts|js|tsx)$' || exit 0

# Run ESLint (non-zero exit = lint errors found)
if ! command -v eslint &>/dev/null; then
  echo "[lint-on-edit] eslint not found in PATH — skipping"
  exit 0
fi

RESULT=$(eslint --no-eslintrc --no-ignore "$FILE" 2>&1 || true)

if [ -n "$RESULT" ]; then
  echo "[lint-on-edit] ESLint output for $FILE:"
  echo "$RESULT"
else
  echo "[lint-on-edit] No lint issues in $FILE"
fi

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
{
  "hooks": {
    "PostToolUse": [
      {
        "matcher": "Write|Edit",
        "hooks": [
          {
            "type": "command",
            "command": "bash ~/.claude/hooks/DP-General-lint-on-edit.sh"
          }
        ]
      }
    ]
  }
}

docs.title: Why this works well
docs.text:
Claude Code reads stdout from hooks as contextual feedback. When ESLint reports a parsing error or a Dialtone-specific rule violation, Claude Code sees it and can immediately propose a fix — without you having to copy-paste the error message.

Use your project's own ESLint config

Replace --no-eslintrc with your project config path to apply Dialpad's actual linting rules: 

eslint --config /path/to/dialpad/web-clients/.eslintrc.js "$FILE"

docs.title: Test Feedback Loop
docs.text: Runs Vitest automatically after Claude Code writes or edits a *.spec.ts or *.test.ts file. Test results are fed back to Claude Code so it can fix failures immediately — creating a tight write → test → fix loop.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text:
1. Fires after every Write or Edit tool call
  2. Checks if the modified file matches *.spec.ts or *.test.ts
  3. Runs vitest run --reporter=verbose scoped to that file
  4. Prints pass/fail output to stdout
  5. Claude Code reads failures and can propose fixes in the same session

docs.title: Hook script
docs.text:
Save to ~/.claude/hooks/DP-General-test-feedback.sh:

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# DP-General-test-feedback.sh
# PostToolUse hook: run Vitest after test file edits and feed results back

set -euo pipefail

INPUT=$(cat)
TOOL=$(echo "$INPUT" | python3 -c "import sys,json; d=json.load(sys.stdin); print(d.get('tool_name',''))")
FILE=$(echo "$INPUT" | python3 -c "import sys,json; d=json.load(sys.stdin); print(d.get('tool_input',{}).get('file_path',''))" 2>/dev/null || echo "")

# Only act on Write/Edit
[[ "$TOOL" == "Write" || "$TOOL" == "Edit" ]] || exit 0

# Only act on test files
echo "$FILE" | grep -qE '\.(spec|test)\.(ts|js)$' || exit 0

if ! command -v vitest &>/dev/null && ! command -v npx &>/dev/null; then
  echo "[test-feedback] vitest not found — skipping"
  exit 0
fi

echo "[test-feedback] Running tests for $FILE..."
RESULT=$(npx vitest run --reporter=verbose "$FILE" 2>&1 || true)
echo "$RESULT"

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
{
  "hooks": {
    "PostToolUse": [
      {
        "matcher": "Write|Edit",
        "hooks": [
          {
            "type": "command",
            "command": "bash ~/.claude/hooks/DP-General-test-feedback.sh"
          }
        ]
      }
    ]
  }
}

docs.title: Expected output pattern
docs.text:
[test-feedback] Running tests for src/components/DialButton.spec.ts...

 FAIL  src/components/DialButton.spec.ts
  ✓ renders with default props (23ms)
  ✗ emits click event when pressed
    AssertionError: expected 0 to equal 1

Test Files  1 failed (1)
Tests       1 failed | 1 passed (2)

Claude Code sees this output and can immediately diagnose the AssertionError and fix the implementation or test.

docs.title: Cross-functional Hooks
docs.text:
• Notify Slack on Session End GA

    View hook

docs.title: Notify Slack on Session End
docs.text: When a Claude Code agent session ends, this hook sends a Slack message to a designated channel with a brief summary of what was produced — keeping the team informed without requiring manual updates.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text:
1. Fires on the Stop event (session ending)
  2. Reads the most recently written output files in the session
  3. Constructs a short summary message
  4. Posts to a Slack webhook URL (set via DIALPAD_SLACK_WEBHOOK env var)

docs.title: Hook script
docs.text:
Save to ~/.claude/hooks/DP-General-notify-slack.sh:

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# DP-General-notify-slack.sh
# Stop hook: post a session completion summary to Slack

set -euo pipefail

WEBHOOK="${DIALPAD_SLACK_WEBHOOK:-}"

if [ -z "$WEBHOOK" ]; then
  echo "[notify-slack] DIALPAD_SLACK_WEBHOOK not set — skipping Slack notification"
  exit 0
fi

# Find recently modified files (last 30 minutes, excluding git/node_modules)
RECENT_FILES=$(find . -maxdepth 4 -newer /tmp/.claude-session-start \
  -name "*.md" -not -path "*/.git/*" -not -path "*/node_modules/*" \
  2>/dev/null | head -5 || echo "")

if [ -z "$RECENT_FILES" ]; then
  SUMMARY="Claude Code session completed — no tracked files modified."
else
  FILE_LIST=$(echo "$RECENT_FILES" | sed 's/^/• /' | tr '\n' '\\n')
  SUMMARY="Claude Code session completed.\n\nFiles produced:\n${FILE_LIST}"
fi

# Get current directory name as project context
PROJECT=$(basename "$(pwd)")

PAYLOAD=$(cat <<JSON
{
  "text": ":robot_face: *[$PROJECT]* ${SUMMARY}"
}
JSON
)

curl -s -X POST -H 'Content-type: application/json' \
  --data "$PAYLOAD" "$WEBHOOK" > /dev/null

echo "[notify-slack] Session summary posted to Slack."

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
{
  "hooks": {
    "Stop": [
      {
        "hooks": [
          {
            "type": "command",
            "command": "bash ~/.claude/hooks/DP-General-notify-slack.sh"
          }
        ]
      }
    ]
  }
}

docs.title: Environment variable
docs.text:
Set your Slack incoming webhook URL in your shell profile:

# Add to ~/.zshrc or ~/.bashrc
export DIALPAD_SLACK_WEBHOOK="https://hooks.slack.com/services/YOUR/WEBHOOK/URL"

Get the webhook URL from your Slack workspace admin or the #ai-playbook channel settings.

docs.title: Example Slack message
docs.text:
🤖 [ai-playbook] Claude Code session completed.

Files produced:
• ./03_skills/pm/DP-General-new-skill.md
• ./04_agents/pm/DP-General-new-agent/CLAUDE.md

docs.title: PM Hooks
docs.text:
• Auto-Save PRD Artifacts GA

    View hook

  • JIRA Push on Session End GA

    View hook

docs.title: Auto-Save PRD Artifacts
docs.text: Automatically copies any markdown file written by Claude Code during a PRD workflow into a timestamped archive folder. Prevents losing work if the session ends unexpectedly and creates an audit trail of PRD versions.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text:
1. Fires after every Write or Edit tool call
  2. Checks if the modified file path matches *prd* or *PRD*
  3. If matched, copies the file to ~/Dialpad-PRD-Archive/YYYY-MM-DD_HH-MM_<filename>
  4. Logs the copy action to stdout (Claude Code receives this as context)

docs.title: Hook script
docs.text:
Save to ~/.claude/hooks/DP-General-auto-save-prd.sh:

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# DP-General-auto-save-prd.sh
# PostToolUse hook: auto-archive PRD files written by Claude Code
#
# Claude Code passes tool output via stdin as JSON:
# { "tool_name": "Write", "tool_input": { "file_path": "...", ... } }

set -euo pipefail

INPUT=$(cat)
TOOL=$(echo "$INPUT" | python3 -c "import sys,json; d=json.load(sys.stdin); print(d.get('tool_name',''))")
FILE=$(echo "$INPUT" | python3 -c "import sys,json; d=json.load(sys.stdin); print(d.get('tool_input',{}).get('file_path',''))" 2>/dev/null || echo "")

# Only act on Write/Edit tool calls
[[ "$TOOL" == "Write" || "$TOOL" == "Edit" ]] || exit 0

# Only act on files matching PRD pattern
echo "$FILE" | grep -qiE 'prd|PRD' || exit 0

ARCHIVE_DIR="$HOME/Dialpad-PRD-Archive"
mkdir -p "$ARCHIVE_DIR"

TIMESTAMP=$(date +"%Y-%m-%d_%H-%M")
BASENAME=$(basename "$FILE")
DEST="$ARCHIVE_DIR/${TIMESTAMP}_${BASENAME}"

cp "$FILE" "$DEST" 2>/dev/null && echo "[auto-save] PRD archived to $DEST"

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Add to .claude/settings.json in your project root:

{
  "hooks": {
    "PostToolUse": [
      {
        "matcher": "Write|Edit",
        "hooks": [
          {
            "type": "command",
            "command": "bash ~/.claude/hooks/DP-General-auto-save-prd.sh"
          }
        ]
      }
    ]
  }
}

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
# 1. Create the hooks directory
mkdir -p ~/.claude/hooks

# 2. Copy the script
cp 10_hooks/pm/DP-General-auto-save-prd.sh ~/.claude/hooks/
chmod +x ~/.claude/hooks/DP-General-auto-save-prd.sh

# 3. Add the configuration to your .claude/settings.json
# (see snippet above)

docs.title: Archive location
docs.text:
Files are saved to ~/Dialpad-PRD-Archive/ with the format: 

2026-03-11_14-30_DP-General-prd-template.md
2026-03-11_15-45_Support-voice-quality-prd.md

docs.title: JIRA Push on Session End
docs.text: When a Claude Code session ends after running /generate-jira-epic or /task-breakdown, this hook reads the last artifact written and prints a reminder with the JIRA MCP command to push the tickets — or automatically invokes the push if the JIRA MCP is configured.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text:
1. Fires on the Stop event (session ending)
  2. Checks if any *epic* or *jira* markdown files were written in the session
  3. If found, prints the JIRA MCP push command as a reminder
  4. If JIRA_AUTO_PUSH=1 env var is set, automatically invokes the Atlassian MCP push

docs.title: Hook script
docs.text:
Save to ~/.claude/hooks/DP-General-jira-push.sh:

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# DP-General-jira-push.sh
# Stop hook: remind PM to push JIRA artifacts after session ends

set -euo pipefail

# Find recently written JIRA/epic files in the current directory
RECENT=$(find . -maxdepth 3 -name "*epic*" -o -name "*jira*" -o -name "*tickets*" \
  | grep -v ".git" | head -5 2>/dev/null || echo "")

if [ -z "$RECENT" ]; then
  exit 0
fi

echo ""
echo "JIRA artifacts detected in this session:"
echo "$RECENT"
echo ""
echo "To push to JIRA, run:"
echo "  claude --mcp-server atlassian \"Create the JIRA epic from the file above\""
echo ""
echo "Or configure the Atlassian MCP in 09_mcps/guides/DP-General-atlassian-setup.md"

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
{
  "hooks": {
    "Stop": [
      {
        "hooks": [
          {
            "type": "command",
            "command": "bash ~/.claude/hooks/DP-General-jira-push.sh"
          }
        ]
      }
    ]
  }
}

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• JIRA MCP Setup
  • Generate JIRA Epic skill

docs.title: UI Hooks
docs.text:
• UI Studio Export Trigger GA

    View hook

docs.title: UI Studio Export Trigger
docs.text: After Claude Code writes a component spec or design handoff document, this hook logs the file to a design artifacts manifest and optionally opens the file in your default editor for review. Designed to work alongside the /component-spec and /spec-to-code-context skills.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text:
1. Fires after every Write or Edit tool call
  2. Checks if the modified file matches *spec*, *handoff*, or *component*
  3. Appends the file path + timestamp to ~/Dialpad-UI-Artifacts/manifest.md
  4. Prints a reminder about the UI Studio MCP for live component token lookups

docs.title: Hook script
docs.text:
Save to ~/.claude/hooks/DP-General-design-studio-export.sh:

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# DP-General-design-studio-export.sh
# PostToolUse hook: log component specs to design artifact manifest

set -euo pipefail

INPUT=$(cat)
TOOL=$(echo "$INPUT" | python3 -c "import sys,json; d=json.load(sys.stdin); print(d.get('tool_name',''))")
FILE=$(echo "$INPUT" | python3 -c "import sys,json; d=json.load(sys.stdin); print(d.get('tool_input',{}).get('file_path',''))" 2>/dev/null || echo "")

# Only act on Write/Edit
[[ "$TOOL" == "Write" || "$TOOL" == "Edit" ]] || exit 0

# Only act on design spec files
echo "$FILE" | grep -qiE 'spec|handoff|component' || exit 0

MANIFEST_DIR="$HOME/Dialpad-UI-Artifacts"
mkdir -p "$MANIFEST_DIR"

TIMESTAMP=$(date +"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M")
echo "- [$TIMESTAMP] $FILE" >> "$MANIFEST_DIR/manifest.md"

echo "[design-studio-export] UI spec logged to $MANIFEST_DIR/manifest.md"
echo "[design-studio-export] Tip: run the UI Studio MCP to validate Dialtone token usage in this spec."

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
{
  "hooks": {
    "PostToolUse": [
      {
        "matcher": "Write|Edit",
        "hooks": [
          {
            "type": "command",
            "command": "bash ~/.claude/hooks/DP-General-design-studio-export.sh"
          }
        ]
      }
    ]
  }
}

docs.title: Manifest format
docs.text:
Each spec write appends a line to ~/Dialpad-UI-Artifacts/manifest.md:

- [2026-03-11 14:30] ./components/DialButton-spec.md
- [2026-03-11 15:12] ./handoff/VoiceQuality-handoff.md
- [2026-03-12 09:45] ./components/CallCard-component-spec.md

This gives you an audit trail of all design specs produced across Claude Code sessions.

docs.title: Design Tenets Framework
docs.text: The Design Tenets framework is a set of seven specific, decisive rules the Design team should use to make trade-off decisions faster and more consistently, especially when there are multiple equally valid approaches and the team needs a clear way to break ties.

docs.title: Principles vs. Tenets
docs.text:
• Design Principles answer: What do we believe in? — foundational beliefs that guide philosophy but often don't resolve trade-offs
  • Design Tenets answer: When we have to choose, which path do we take? — specific rules that intentionally choose a consistent trade-off

docs.title: 1. Design systems, not surfaces
docs.text:
Features should exist where people need them, not where the team decides to place them. Design should follow the user's path rather than forcing users to follow the product's structure.

This means distributing functionality across multiple contexts — thinking about ingress points, egress points, and natural moments of need. This is especially important for Dialpad where a single person may move between personas (supervisor in one contact center, agent in another).

Tiebreaker: One dedicated location vs. distributed across multiple contexts? → Choose distribution.

docs.title: 2. Opt into complexity
docs.text:
Power lives in the product, but not all power should live on the surface. Users should discover advanced features when they need them, without being overwhelmed upfront.

Common workflows should feel frictionless. Advanced or rare workflows can require discovery. This is especially relevant as Dialpad ships more AI capabilities.

Tiebreaker: Add a powerful capability to the core experience vs. stay simple? → Choose simplicity.

docs.title: 3. Transparency is non-negotiable
docs.text:
Complexity should be hidden in the implementation, not in the interface. Users deserve to understand what is happening and why.

This is especially important for AI features — the interface must clearly communicate inputs, outputs, and enough reasoning for users to calibrate trust.

Tiebreaker: Hide system complexity vs. show the truth? → Show the truth.

docs.title: 4. Anticipation over reaction
docs.text:
The moment a user finishes an action and has to hunt for the next step, friction enters. The product should surface what users are most likely to need next.

AI can significantly enhance anticipation by understanding context and conversation history to surface relevant actions before the user asks.

Tiebreaker: Wait for explicit user input vs. surface predicted needs? → Anticipate.

docs.title: 5. Friction is deliberate
docs.text:
Not everything should be frictionless. Important actions should feel important.

Everyday actions should be fast and effortless. Rare, risky, or high-impact actions should introduce friction (confirmation steps, re-authentication, review screens, explicit warnings).

Tiebreaker: Smooth and effortless always vs. intentional friction for rare actions? → Add deliberate friction.

docs.title: 6. Customize to lock-in
docs.text:
In a crowded market, the moat is not features but the time and energy users invest in making the product their own.

As users personalize — saved configurations, custom AI instructions, learned preferences, custom skills — they build muscle memory and fluency that creates switching costs.

Tiebreaker: One-size-fits-all vs. letting the user make it their own? → Invest in customization.

docs.title: 7. Capability without clarity is just noise
docs.text:
A powerful feature without understanding is worse than not shipping the feature at all.

Users need to understand when a feature is useful, how it works, and what it's good at or not good at. This is especially true for AI features. Shipping more slowly with clarity is preferable to shipping quickly and creating confusion.

Tiebreaker: Ship powerful feature quickly vs. hold until refined? → Wait for clarity.

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• FY26 Q4 Design All Hands Recording
  • FY26 Q4 Design All Hands Deck
  • Lenny's Podcast: 35 years of product design wisdom from Apple, Disney, Pinterest and beyond

For feedback or questions about Design Tenets, contact Joshua Hynes.

Source

docs.title: Design Tools Repository
docs.text:
Design Tools is our shared home for building, sharing, and shipping tools made by designers, for designers. It lives in a single GitHub repository — a monorepo where all tools sit side by side, but each one is completely independent.

  • Website: dialpad.github.io/design-tools
  • Repository: github.com/dialpad/design-tools

Access

The Design Tools website and CSS Converter require Dialpad org access. Links will only work for authenticated Dialpad team members.

docs.title: What's Inside
docs.text: Tool What it does Type CSS Converter Converts between CSS, Tailwind, and Dialtone tokens instantly Web app GCP Cloud Functions Backend services that power AI features like GPT, Claude, and copy generation Serverless APIs Claude Skills Reusable AI workflows you can drop into any project Config files AI Clients Custom GPTs, Google Gems, Rovo agents, and NotebookLM configs AI configurations Text-to-AI A FigJam plugin that extracts text content for AI processing FigJam plugin

docs.title: Repository Structure
docs.text:
design-tools/
├── css-converter/       ← Web app for CSS/Tailwind/Dialtone conversion
├── gcp-functions/       ← Cloud functions (GPT, Claude, copywriting)
├── claude-skills/       ← AI workflows for Claude Code
├── ai-agents/           ← Custom GPTs, Gems, Rovos, NotebookLMs
├── text-to-ai/          ← FigJam plugin
├── src/                 ← The Design Tools website itself
├── README.md            ← Technical overview
├── DEVELOPER_GUIDE.md   ← Full dev setup guide
└── CONTRIBUTING.md      ← How to contribute

docs.title: 1. Clone the repo
docs.text:
git clone git@github.com:dialpad/design-tools.git
cd design-tools

For a single tool only (faster download):

git clone --filter=blob:none --no-checkout git@github.com:dialpad/design-tools.git
cd design-tools
git sparse-checkout init --cone
git sparse-checkout set css-converter
git checkout main

docs.title: 2. Install and run
docs.text:
cd css-converter
npm install
npm run dev
# Open http://localhost:5173

For Claude Skills — no install needed. Just copy the skill files into any project's .claude/skills/ folder.

docs.title: Building Something New
docs.text:
1. Propose it — Create a GitHub Issue describing what the tool does and how it would be deployed
  2. Create your folder — Root-level folder with its own README.md and CHANGELOG.md
  3. Build and document — Include install, run, deploy, and troubleshooting instructions
  4. Submit a PR — Push your branch and open a pull request

docs.title: Branch Naming
docs.text:
css-converter/add-new-tokens
gcp-functions/update-cors
claude-skills/add-new-skill
your-tool/initial-setup

docs.title: Quick Reference
docs.text:
I want to... Do this Browse all tools Visit dialpad.github.io/design-tools Convert CSS to Dialtone Visit dialpad.github.io/css-converter Clone the repo git clone git@github.com:dialpad/design-tools.git Run CSS Converter locally cd css-converter && npm install && npm run dev Create a new tool Open a GitHub Issue first, then create a folder Get help Dialpad #design-eng-support or open a GitHub Issue

Source

This content is sourced from Design Tools on Confluence.

docs.title: Using Figma at Dialpad 101
docs.text:
Figma is our main tool for designing product experiences at Dialpad. This section gives you everything you need to get started — from starter files to the libraries that help keep things consistent across projects.

Using these shared libraries is required — it ensures that everyone's working from the same structure and speaking the same visual language. That said, don't hold back — bring your creativity into every project.

docs.title: Primary Design Tool
docs.text:
Our primary design tool is Figma. As a Product Designer or Product Design Manager, you will have access to Figma and the Dialpad team projects. You can access Figma via Okta.

Anyone else in Dialpad can access your files when you share a file URL or invite them via email. We ask that you only grant non-Designers "view" permissions. Anyone given "edit" access is a paid seat and must be approved by their department head. A user with "view" permissions can comment on and inspect design files.

docs.title: Access & Permissions
docs.text:
Figma has four access levels, also called Permissions. Permissions can be set at a team, project, and file level.

Role Description Editors Only Product Designers and Product Design Managers in the UX department are editors. Editors have "can edit" access to projects and files unless otherwise changed. Viewers Has "view" access to any file within a project. If invited, viewers are able to be granted editor permission. This is a paid seat and should only be done with a department head's written approval. Viewer-Restricted A viewer role, but locked and can only be upgraded to an editor role if promoted by an admin. Admins Has the ability to manage users, teams, and projects. Owners Editors or admins who create a team, project, or file will be the default owner.

docs.title: Figma Utility Files
docs.text:
These Figma utility files help structure your design work while making it easier for others to understand your thinking and follow the story behind your decisions.

File Description Link Project File Template A ready-to-go file structure that keeps designs organized, consistent, and easy to collaborate on. View in Figma Annotation Library Visual callouts in Figma that explain design choices and connect visuals to intent. View in Figma Figma Documentation Patterns Structured methods in Figma to clearly show flows, interactions, and design rationale. View in Figma

docs.title: Dialtone Figma Libraries
docs.text:
These libraries are part of Dialpad's design system, Dialtone, and are essential for ensuring consistency across brand, user experience, and design in every project.

Library Description Link DT9 Component Library Core UI building blocks used across Dialpad products. View in Figma DT9 Design Tokens Core styles like colors, spacing, and typography for theming. Dialtone docs DT9 Icon Library Collection of standardized icons for consistent visual language. View in Figma Spot Illustrations Branded artwork used to enhance empty states and visuals. Dialtone docs App Components App-specific components tailored to Dialpad's products. View in Figma DT9 Design Tokens (Dark Mode) Dark mode variants of core design tokens. Dialtone docs

docs.title: Git & GitHub for Designers
docs.text:
If you've ever stared at a terminal wondering why your changes disappeared, or panicked when GitHub said "merge conflict", you're not alone. This guide answers the most common questions designers ask about Git and GitHub.

Git is the tool that lets multiple people work on the same codebase without overwriting each other's work. GitHub is where that code lives online. Together, they're how we collaborate on Design Studio, Beacon, Dialtone, and every other repo at Dialpad.

docs.title: Find Your Level
docs.text:
The guide is split into levels, from setting up your account to advanced workflows. Each level has instructions for both GitHub Desktop and the Terminal.

Level What it covers Start here if... 0 - Account & Access GitHub account, org access, installing Git and GitHub Desktop "How do I get set up with GitHub?" 1 - First Time Setup Cloning repos, SSH keys, tokens, dependencies "How do I get a repo running on my machine?" 2 - Branches Creating, naming, switching, updating "How do I create a branch to start working?" 3 - Making Changes Committing, pushing, commit messages "How do I save my changes and upload to GitHub?" 4 - Pull Requests Creating PRs, previews, reviews, merging "How do I share my work and get it reviewed?" 5 - Merge Conflicts What they are, how to fix them "GitHub says there are conflicts, what now?" 6 - Common Mistakes Wrong branch, broken previews, huge PRs "I messed something up, how do I fix it?" 7 - Advanced Worktrees, cherry-pick, rebase "How do I work in parallel or move specific commits?"

docs.title: Repo-Specific Docs
docs.text:
Branch naming, PR workflows, and setup steps vary by repo. For repo-specific steps:

Repo Docs Design Studio README and docs/ folder (internal repo) Beacon getting-started.md and CONTRIBUTING.md Design Tools README Dialtone Engineering Onboarding Guide Firespotter setup-firespotter skill in Claude Code

docs.title: Jira for Product Design
docs.text:
Starting April 2023, we decided to use Jira as our main tool for project management. The goal is to:

  • Eliminate effort duplication for Designers
  • Adopt practices that allow us to have updated information in real time
  • Have a single source of truth for the Design Team to have a bird-eye view on all in-flight and backlog projects

This source of truth empowers Designers to prioritize, unblock, optimize, and communicate their workflow better.

Kanban boards:

  • Product Designer view
  • Epic view

docs.title: Card Definitions
docs.text: Card Description Jira Statuses  TO DO Tickets in the Designer's backlog TO DO, NEEDS DEFINITION  BLOCKED Assigned but waiting on dependencies BLOCKED  ACTIVE WORK Currently being worked on IN PROGRESS, CODE REVIEW  DESIGN REVIEW Needs review / being reviewed DESIGN REVIEW  QA Designer assigned for QA READY FOR TESTING  DONE Work completed DONE, CLOSED, READY FOR PRODUCTION, NOT DOING

docs.title: Status Definitions
docs.text: Status Description Requirements NEEDS TRIAGE Vague definition and unclear scope Brief description, Priority BACKLOG Scope is clear, ready to be worked on Complete description with acceptance criteria, Priority, design-required label, Story point estimation TO DO Prioritized for current sprint All BACKLOG requirements + Assignee BLOCKED Clear scope but dependency not resolved Brief description, Linked blocking issue IN PROGRESS Work in progress All BACKLOG requirements + Assignee DESIGN REVIEW Being reviewed by another designer All BACKLOG requirements + Assignee CLOSED Acceptance criteria achieved All requirements + design-complete label

docs.title: Filter Used
docs.text:
project IN (Dialpad, "Dialpad Meetings", "Mobile", "Dialtone", "Dialpad Design")
AND ((assignee IN (membersOf("Product Design")) OR labels IN (design-required)))
AND issuetype != Epic

docs.title: Jira Issue Types
docs.text:
Issue Type Description Scope Length Initiative / Plan Large project spanning several quarters Not necessarily defined Undefined Epic Part of an initiative, collection of stories Defined <= Quarter Story / Design Story Small portion of work Defined <= Sprint Spike Timeboxed investigation to gather info Defined <= Sprint Bug Reflects an existing error Defined Undefined Sub task Portion of a story Defined <= Sprint

Key rules

  • All tickets you create and work on must belong to an epic
  • Use the design-required label for active design work
  • Switch to design-done label when design work is complete
  • Epics should not be assigned to individual designers

Source

This content is sourced from Jira for Product Design on Confluence.

docs.title: Tools and Software for Research
docs.text:
We have the following tools and software available to perform User Research. They can be used for both getting a list of users / creating an audience and contacting them.

Tool What can I find there? Steward Links Amplitude Event-based tracking in clients. Android, iOS, and Dialpad Everywhere. Every new product should have a tag for tracking. Data team Taxonomy Appointment Slots Google Calendar feature replacing Calendly. External users can book a slot in your calendar. N/A --- Chameleon (license required) Create campaigns and send in-app messages to audiences, inviting users to interviews. Growth Marketing --- Clari Lost Report Weekly email report with churned customers due to product limitations and estimated lost deals. PMs Email inbox Competitive Intelligence (CI) Monthly report summarizing the competitive landscape: news, updates, and deep dives on competitors. PMs CI Wiki CSR Internal tool for managing Dialpad accounts and settings. Find CSMs assigned to companies. IT CSR Database CSM Team Customer Success team --- account managers for Enterprise accounts. CSM --- Dialpad Channels App feedback from users via Google Form, captured in #feedback-from-users channels. PMs Feedback spreadsheet Dovetail Research repository --- wiki for user research insights and learnings. AI features help find insights on specific topics. Design --- Experimentation / A/B Tests Repository for user testing experiments run by the Growth team. Growth Marketing --- Highspot Dialpad resource hub with information used mostly by Sales and Rev orgs. Marketing --- Marketo Stores user data from SFDC. Creates email, newsletter, and other campaigns for contacting users at scale. MarkOps team Marketo fields Google BigQuery (special permissions required) Primary database where all Dialpad info is stored. Requires SQL knowledge. ENG --- Segment (license required) Gathers data from many sources (Amplitude, GBQ, etc.). Create audiences and contact users through other platforms. Growth Marketing Segment CDP PMs Monthly Metric Monthly call where PMs share Adoption, Usage, and Revenue performance for each product area. PMs Meeting notes Secoda AI Fed with internal data sources, works as an LLM --- ask questions and get query-backed answers. Data team --- Tableau Data visualization tool with dashboards from Google BigQuery. Data team User View Dataset UserVoice Platform managed by CS team to gather product/feature ideas submitted by customers. Customer Success --- Wootric Captures NPS feedback from users. Request access through Okta. PMs ---

Before contacting users

Always review the Customer and Partner Exception List and contact their CSMs if they are listed there.

docs.title: UX Heuristics & Scorecards
docs.text: Heuristic evaluations are a method for discovering potential usability problems within user interfaces and evaluating compliance with industry-standard usability principles.

docs.title: Categories
docs.text:
Value Heuristic Simple and obvious — Achieve the same outcome in fewer steps, or a better outcome in the same steps Match between system and the real world — Experiences should speak the users' language using familiar words, phrases, and concepts rather than system-oriented terms. Aesthetics and minimalistic design — Interfaces should not contain irrelevant information. Every extra unit of info competes with the relevant units. Effortless — Help the user get to the expected outcome as quickly as possible Consistency and standards — Users should not have to wonder whether different words, situations, or actions mean the same thing. Error prevention — Good error messages are important, but the best designs prevent problems from occurring in the first place. Scalable — Make sure the workflow works at scale Flexibility and efficiency of use — Shortcuts may speed up interaction for expert users. Recognize, diagnose, recover from errors — Error messages should be in plain language, precisely indicating the problem and suggesting solutions. Discoverable — The user can easily discover the feature Recognition rather than recall — Minimize user memory load by making elements, actions, and options visible. Visibility of system status — Designs continually inform users on their status through appropriate and timely feedback. Help and documentation — When necessary, users are given help to complete their tasks. User control and freedom — Allow users to exit a flow or undo an action and go back to previous system state.

We also track: Time on task and Number of clicks (if applicable).

docs.title: Scoring
docs.text: Grade Score Summary Ease Experience GRADE A 5 Exceeds expectations — Heuristic is met in every way and optimized. Users may report the task is delightful. Extremely easy Extremely good GRADE B 4 Somewhat exceeds expectations — Heuristic is met almost in every way with no more than minor exceptions. Successful Good GRADE C 3 Meets expectations — Heuristic is mostly met. Minor workarounds may be needed. Okay Okay GRADE D 2 Falls short of expectations — Heuristic is lacking in significant ways leading to user frustration. Difficult Bad GRADE F 1 Does not meet expectations — Heuristic isn't met. Task may be impossible to complete. Very difficult Poor

docs.title: For Product Designers
docs.text:
• Identifying Usability Issues — Uncover challenges within the user experience workflow
  • Early Problem Detection — Catch issues during the design phase, saving time and resources
  • Objective Evaluation — Based on established heuristics, providing objective assessment
  • Iterative Design Improvement — Feedback enables iterating and refining workflows
  • Enhanced User Satisfaction — Well-designed workflows lead to improved satisfaction

docs.title: For Product Design Leadership
docs.text:
• Ensuring consistency across design guidelines and standards
  • Validating design decisions and gaining alignment insights
  • Improving overall product quality

docs.title: For PMs, ENG, and Stakeholders
docs.text:
• Aligning with Industry Best Practices
  • Saving Time and Resources through early issue identification
  • Improving Collaboration across cross-functional teams
  • Enhancing Brand Reputation through quality user experiences

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:

  • Scorecards Archive (Google Drive)

Source

docs.title: UI Tenets Framework
docs.text: The UI Tenets framework is a set of seven specific, decisive rules the UI team should use to make trade-off decisions faster and more consistently, especially when there are multiple equally valid approaches and the team needs a clear way to break ties.

docs.title: Principles vs. Tenets
docs.text:
• UI Principles answer: What do we believe in? — foundational beliefs that guide philosophy but often don't resolve trade-offs
  • UI Tenets answer: When we have to choose, which path do we take? — specific rules that intentionally choose a consistent trade-off

docs.title: 1. UI systems, not surfaces
docs.text:
Features should exist where people need them, not where the team decides to place them. UI should follow the user's path rather than forcing users to follow the product's structure.

This means distributing functionality across multiple contexts — thinking about ingress points, egress points, and natural moments of need. This is especially important for Dialpad where a single person may move between personas (supervisor in one contact center, agent in another).

Tiebreaker: One dedicated location vs. distributed across multiple contexts? → Choose distribution.

docs.title: 2. Opt into complexity
docs.text:
Power lives in the product, but not all power should live on the surface. Users should discover advanced features when they need them, without being overwhelmed upfront.

Common workflows should feel frictionless. Advanced or rare workflows can require discovery. This is especially relevant as Dialpad ships more AI capabilities.

Tiebreaker: Add a powerful capability to the core experience vs. stay simple? → Choose simplicity.

docs.title: 3. Transparency is non-negotiable
docs.text:
Complexity should be hidden in the implementation, not in the interface. Users deserve to understand what is happening and why.

This is especially important for AI features — the interface must clearly communicate inputs, outputs, and enough reasoning for users to calibrate trust.

Tiebreaker: Hide system complexity vs. show the truth? → Show the truth.

docs.title: 4. Anticipation over reaction
docs.text:
The moment a user finishes an action and has to hunt for the next step, friction enters. The product should surface what users are most likely to need next.

AI can significantly enhance anticipation by understanding context and conversation history to surface relevant actions before the user asks.

Tiebreaker: Wait for explicit user input vs. surface predicted needs? → Anticipate.

docs.title: 5. Friction is deliberate
docs.text:
Not everything should be frictionless. Important actions should feel important.

Everyday actions should be fast and effortless. Rare, risky, or high-impact actions should introduce friction (confirmation steps, re-authentication, review screens, explicit warnings).

Tiebreaker: Smooth and effortless always vs. intentional friction for rare actions? → Add deliberate friction.

docs.title: 6. Customize to lock-in
docs.text:
In a crowded market, the moat is not features but the time and energy users invest in making the product their own.

As users personalize — saved configurations, custom AI instructions, learned preferences, custom skills — they build muscle memory and fluency that creates switching costs.

Tiebreaker: One-size-fits-all vs. letting the user make it their own? → Invest in customization.

docs.title: 7. Capability without clarity is just noise
docs.text:
A powerful feature without understanding is worse than not shipping the feature at all.

Users need to understand when a feature is useful, how it works, and what it's good at or not good at. This is especially true for AI features. Shipping more slowly with clarity is preferable to shipping quickly and creating confusion.

Tiebreaker: Ship powerful feature quickly vs. hold until refined? → Wait for clarity.

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• FY26 Q4 UI All Hands Recording
  • FY26 Q4 UI All Hands Deck
  • Lenny's Podcast: 35 years of product design wisdom from Apple, Disney, Pinterest and beyond

For feedback or questions about UI Tenets, contact Joshua Hynes.
